messages.content: You are a DREAM causation annotation assistant. Given an NHTSA crash case input, output only the final standardized DREAM causation chains. Use the approved code set and exact labels. Do not explain your reasoning. Preserve the final line-break format.

Please convert the following NHTSA case into the final DREAM causation chain output.

[INPUT]
Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This two vehicle daytime collision occurred during morning hours on a Thursday. The collision occurred on a curve, wet arterial roadway with traffic in both directions. The pre-crash roadway for both vehicles was an uphill grade of 8%, paved with bituminous. The roadway has a posted speed limit of 48 kmph (30 mph). It was raining at this time and the roadways were wet. Vehicle 1 (V 1), a 1995 Toyota Tercel coupe, driven by a 28 year-old female was traveling north/east on the roadway in lane 1. Vehicle 2 (V 2), 1992 Geo Prism sedan, driven by a 62 year-old male was traveling south/west in lane 1. V 1 was attempting to pass two cyclists on the right over a double yellow line, on an incline and around a curve. V 1 was coming in opposite direction again on an incline and around a curve. V 1 impacted V 2 head on causing them to rotate clockwise just a little bit. Both vehicles came to rest facing the direction they were originally heading. Both vehicles were towed from the scene, Driver of V 2 was transported to a local medical facility. Neither driver was willing to answer questions on scene and could not be contacted for an interview. Upon researching the scene, there is no sight line for either vehicle approaching the crest of the hill. The pre-event movement for V 1 was coded going straight. The critical pre-crash event was coded This vehicle traveling: Over lane line on left side of travel lane. The critical reason for the critical event assigned to this driver as Driver related factor: "Illegal maneuver". (crossing over double yellow line). The pre-event movement for V 2 was coded going straight. The critical pre-crash event was coded Other Motor Vehicle in Lane: Traveling in opposite direction. The critical reason for the critical event is not coded to this vehicle.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 1:  Decision, Roadway, Weather/Environment present problem 
Vehicle 2:  Roadway, Weather/Environment present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Going straight
 2 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Over the lane line on left side of travel lane
 2 ：Other Motor Vehicle in Lane- Traveling in opposite direction

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Driver Related Factor- Illegal maneuver
 2 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.1 - Too early action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation
Genotype: D1 - Priority error

Vehicle 2 (V2)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary The two-vehicle crash occurred in the middle of a four-way intersection. The northbound roadway (Vehicle 1) was a two lane undivided asphalt, dry, and level roadway. The eastbound roadway (Vehicle 2) was also a two lane undivided roadway; however, there were metal trolley tracks planted in cement on both sides of the roadway. The posted speed limit was 40 kmph (25mph) for all directions. The intersection was controlled by signalized traffic lights without pedestrian crossing for all directions. The crash occurred in the early afternoon on a weekend. There were no adverse weather conditions and traffic flow was normal. Vehicle 2, a 1995 Mazda Millenia, was traveling eastbound, speed unknown, and was crossing through the intersection when its front contacted the left front side of Vehicle 1, a 1995 Honda Accord. The police report indicated that it was unknown as to which vehicle had the right of way; however, scene evidence, vehicle damage, and final resting points indicated that Vehicle 2 ran the red light. The driver of Vehicle 2, a 30-year old female, was issued several citations. One of the violations was for driving a motor vehicle without a valid driver's license. The other was for driving with illegal movement and another for not having the vehicle properly insured. The driver of Vehicle 2 was unavailable for an interview. She was taken from the scene to the hospital with minor injuries. The driver of Vehicle 1, a 24-year old female, stated that she had the right of way and had the green light. She stated that Vehicle 2 drove through the red light and struck her vehicle on the left side. This impact spun Vehicle 1 in a clockwise direction and its front left side contacted a pole on the southeast corner of the intersection. The driver of Vehicle 1 was not injured. The Critical Pre-Crash Event for Vehicle 1 was coded as other vehicle encroachment from crossing street, across path. The Critical Reason was not coded to the driver of Vehicle 1. An associated factor code to this driver was unfamiliarity with the roadway - this was the first time she had driven on it. The Critical Pre-crash Event for Vehicle 2 was coded as this vehicle crossing over (passing through) intersection. The Critical Reason was coded as an illegal maneuver. Associated factors coded to this driver include several prescription medications for the treatment of asthma. Other associated factors were unknown due to the lack of an interview.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 1:  Driver Inexperience present problem 
Vehicle 2:  Decision, Driver Physical, Drugs present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Going straight
 2 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Crossing over (passing through) intersection
 2 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Crossing over (passing through) intersection

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle
 2 ：Driver Related Factor- Illegal maneuver

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation
Genotype: F6 - Insufficient skills/knowledge

Vehicle 2 (V2)
Phenotype: A1.1 - Too early action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation
Genotype: D1 - Priority error

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This two vehicle crash occurred shortly before noon during daylight hours at the intersection of a four-lane, two-way, east-west, undivided roadway with a three-lane, one-way northbound roadway. The intersection was controlled by a tri-color traffic signal with pedestrian controls. The roadways were straight, level, concrete with a posted speed of 56 kmph (35 mph) northbound and 48 kmph (30 mph) east and westbound. the road was dry and the sky was clear. Traffic flow was moderate. Vehicle one (V 1), a 1998 Mitsubishi Montero Sport sport utility vehicle driven by a 28 year-old female was eastbound in lane two. Vehicle two (V 2), a 1993 Ford F-150 pick-up driven by a 32 year-old male was traveling northbound in lane two. V 2 entered the intersection without the right-of-way and was struck on the left side by the front of V 1. V 2 rotated counterclockwise and came to rest in the roadway north of the intersection. The post impact movement of V 1 is unknown. V 1 was pushed to a nearby parking lot by the fire department prior to researchers arrival. V 1 was towed from the scene,driver refused to be interviewed. The driver of V 2 advised that he was traveling between 32-48 kmph (20-30 mph) at the time of impact. He did not see V 1 prior to the collision and took no evasive action. V 2 was driven from the scene. The critical pre-crash event for V2 was coded: this vehicle traveling, crossing over/passing through intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was driver related factor- unknown recognition error. The critical pre-crash event for V1 was coded: other vehicle encroachment; from crossing street- across path. The Critical reason not coded to this vehicle. Associated factors for the driver of V 2 was illegal maneuver, failed to stop for traffic control device. also inadequate surveillance- the driver of V 2 did not report seeing V 1 prior to impact.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 2:  Decision, Surveillance present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Going straight
 2 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Other Vehicle Encroachment- From crossing street, across path
 2 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Crossing over (passing through) intersection

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle
 2 ：Driver Related Factor- Unknown recognition error

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action

Vehicle 2 (V2)
Phenotype: A1.1 - Too early action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation
Genotype: D1 - Priority error
Genotype: B1 - Missed observation

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary The two-vehicle crash occurred at the intersection of a three-lane divided roadway and a six-lane divided roadway. For westbound traffic there is two travel lanes. For northbound traffic there is three travel lanes. The westbound roadway was dry concrete with a +7.4% Grade and posted speed limit of 48 kph (30 mph). Westbound vehicles entering the intersection are controlled by stop signs located on both sides of the westbound lanes. The northbound roadway was dry asphalt with a -2.5% grade and a posted speed limit of 56 kph (35 mph). Traffic was light at the time of the accident. This crash occurred during daylight hours with clear skies. Vehicle one (V1), a 2002 Dodge Stratus with two occupants, was westbound in the second travel lane attempting to turn into the southbound lanes. Vehicle two (V2), a 2006 Ford Crown Victoria with one occupant, was northbound in the second travel lane. V1 entered the intersection and the front of V2 impacted the left side of V1. V1 rotated clockwise and came to rest facing northwest, off the roadway to the west. V2 rotated counterclockwise and came to rest facing northwest in the intersection. Both vehicles were towed due to damage. The two occupants in V1 were treated at the scene. The driver of V2 was transported. The driver of V1, a seventeen-year-old female was unable to be interviewed. The right front passenger was also a seventeen-year-old female. The driver's occupation was listed as a student on the officer's report. It was not determined if the driver stopped at the stop sign before proceeding into the intersection. The critical pre-crash event for V1 was coded as "this vehicle traveling: turning left at intersection." The critical reason for the critical pre-crash event was attributed to V1 and was coded as "driver related factor: type of driver error unknown." The driver of V2, a forty-eight year old female, was heading to work from home at the time of the crash. This driver had been driving for about twenty minutes. This driver stated that she never saw the other vehicle coming. This driver drives this vehicle daily and over this road on a daily basis. The critical pre-crash event for V2 was coded as "other vehicle encroachment: from crossing street turning into opposite direction." The critical reason for the critical pre-crash event for vehicle two was coded as "not coded to this vehicle."
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 2:  Decision, Surveillance present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Unknown
 2 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Turning left at intersection
 2 ：Other Vehicle Encroachment- From crossing street, turning into opposite direction

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Driver Related Factor- Type of driver error unknown
 2 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.1 - Too early action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation

Vehicle 2 (V2)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This crash occurred on a weekday night in the southbound lanes of a high-occupancy, high volume interstate. There were five southbound lanes with paved emergency shoulders on both sides of the roadway. There were concrete barriers on both sides of the roadway. The bituminous roadway was dry, straight, and level with a posted speed limit of 105kmph (65mph). The traffic flow was normal and there were no adverse atmospheric conditions at the time of the crash. V1, an unidentified vehicle, was southbound in lane one traveling at an unknown speed. V2, a 1997 Toyota corolla, was southbound in lane two ahead of V1 traveling at 113kmph (70 mph). For unknown reasons V1 began to change lanes to the left into lane two. As V1 entered lane two his front left front corner made contact with the right rear of V2. The impact caused V2 to lose control of his vehicle and depart the roadway where he struck the concrete barrier with his right side causing his vehicle to roll left on quarter turn. V1 fled the scene without stopping. V1 fled the scene and could not be located. No subsequent information could be obtained regarding the either the vehicle or the driver. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was coded this vehicle traveling, over the left lane line. The Critical Event was unknown as the driver fled the scene. There are no known associated factors for this V1 was the driver could not be interviewed nor the vehicle inspected. V2 was driven by a 23 year-old on his commute to work. He stated in the interview that he was driving to work with the cruise control on when all of the sudden was hit from behind by an unidentified vehicle. The impact caused him to lose control of the vehicle. He tried to correct the loss of control by steering left and hitting his brakes but was unable to regain control of the vehicle. The driver was treated on scene for non-incapacitating injuries and V2 was towed due to damage. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was coded other vehicle encroachment, from the adjacent lane over the right lane. The Critical Reason was not coded to this vehicle. There were no associative factors related to the driver and he was not thought to have contributed to the crash.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 2:  Drugs present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Going straight
 2 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Over the lane line on left side of travel lane
 2 ：Other Vehicle Encroachment- From adjacent lane (same direction) - over right lane line

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Unknown Reason - Unknown reason for critical event
 2 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.1 - Too early action

Vehicle 2 (V2)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary The crash occurred on a north/south two-lane urban residential roadway. The road formed a T-intersection with a two-lane east/west road. Approaching the intersection it had a negative 6% grade and there was no stop sign or painted lane lines present on the lightly traveled road. The posted speed limit was 48kmph (30mph). Conditions were dark with streetlights illuminating on a weekday night. Vehicle #1 was a 1996 Lincoln Continental traveling northbound. It passed straight through the T-intersection and struck a house that was 13 meters north of the roadway with its front. There was a negative 16% grade between the road and house contacted. The vehicle came to rest against the house and was towed with minor damage. Vehicle #1 was driven by a 55-year old male that was belted with deployed frontal airbags. He was not injured in the crash. The driver was a cancer patient and had taken morphine 2-3 hours before the crash. In his interview with researcher he said he intended to turn left at the intersection and was not sure what happened, but he believed that he blacked out from the medication. However, at the scene researchers overheard the driver tell police that he had looked down to retrieve lottery tickets from the seat beside him when he passed through the intersection. He had driven from his residence, which was approximately 100 meters west of the crash scene, to a convenient store less than a mile away and was returning when he crashed. Police administered a field sobriety test and charged the driver with driving under the influence (5th offense), although alcohol was not involved in this crash. He was also charged with driving on revoked license, no insurance, no registration, and failure to stop at a stop sign. The Critical Precrash Event for Vehicle #1 was this vehicle end departure. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was internal distraction. Associated factors included the non-driving activity of retrieving object from seat and fatigue. Other physical factor annotated chemotherapy patient was also coded. The driver was wearing glasses to correct a farsighted condition and was taking approximately 12 cancer drugs and morphine.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 1:  Distraction, Driver Fatigue, Driver Physical, Drugs present problem 

Output Text 2:
 Movement Prior to Critical Crash Envelope
 1 ：Going straight

 Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：This Vehicle Traveling- End departure

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Driver Related Factor- Internal distraction

Vehicle 1 (V1)
Phenotype: A1.3 - No action
Genotype: C2 - Misjudgement of situation
Genotype: E3 - Fatigue
Genotype: E4 - Under the influence of substances
Genotype: E4.3 - Medication
Genotype: E2 - Inattention
Genotype: E2.3 - Non driving-related distracters inside vehicle

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary The crash occurred in the morning of a weekday in the middle of a four-way high-volume intersection. The intersection consisted of a north/south were two southbound six-lane undivided roadway and a divided east/west roadway controlled by a properly functioning traffic signal. The bituminous north/south roadway had a posted speed limit of 72kmph (45 mph), a level grade in the southbound direction, and a 2% downhill grade in the northbound direction. The roadway curved slightly to the east and was wet due to the rain that was falling at the time of the crash. The traffic flow was normal. V1, a 2002 Kia Sportage, was southbound in lane two approaching the intersection where it intended to go straight. V2, a 2005 Honda Odyssey minivan, was stopped in the northbound lane three at the intersection where it was intended to turn left. V1 failed to stop for the red light and continued into the intersection as V2 began its left turn. The front of V1 struck the left plane of V2. Both vehicles came to rest in the intersection near the point of impact. The driver of V1, a 65-year old female, was uninjured. She stated that she was running an errand from work and accidentally drove through a red light. She braked and steered right to avoid a vehicle turning left in front of her, but was unable to avoid a collision. Her estimated travel and impact speeds were 40kmph (25 mph) and 32kmph (20 mph), respectively. The driver was taking medications for diabetes and high blood pressure. The compact utility was not towed due to police-reported damage. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was coded this vehicle traveling, passing through (crossing over) intersection. The Critical Reason was not coded to this vehicle. Associated factors for V1 include, inadequate surveillance, a tire deficiency (low tread), as well as the rain and wet roads. The driver of V2, a 31-year old female, was uninjured. There was an 18-month old passenger who was also uninjured. She stated that she was taking her daughter to day care and turning left at an intersection with a protected green arrow. Another vehicle then came across the intersection and struck her before she had a chance to react. She had waited for more than 10 seconds to turn left at the intersection. Her estimated travel speed was 24kmph (15 mph). The minivan was not towed due to police-reported damage. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was this vehicle traveling turning left at an intersection. The Critical Reason was coded as a driver related factor, false assumption of other's action. The driver of V2 assumed that V1 would yield the right of way. Associated factors include the rain and wet roads.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 1:  Drugs, Roadway, Surveillance, Vehicle, Weather/Environment present problem 
Vehicle 2:  Decision, Roadway, Weather/Environment present problem

 2 ：Stopped in traffic lane

 2 ：This Vehicle Traveling- Turning left at intersection

 Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event
 1 ：Not coded to this vehicle- Critical reason not coded to this vehicle
 2 ：Driver Related Factor- False assumption of other's actions

[INPUT]
Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This crash occurred in the northbound lanes of a five-lane non-divided roadway. There were two northbound lanes and two southbound lanes with a left turn lane in the middle. The crash occurred when Vehicle 1 a 1998 Jaguar XJS Cabriolet traveling east crossed over the center line of the roadway and failed to stop for the red traffic light traveling into the path of Vehicle 2 a 1996 Chevrolet S-10 pick-up. V2's front contacted the right side of V1. V1 then rotated counter clockwise side slapping V2 traveling into a restaurant parking lot, and contacting posts in front of the restaurant then coming to final rest in the lot. V2 then rotated clockwise and contacted a fire hydrant with the back rim and rear bumper cap on the left rear side of the pick-up. Then traveling back onto the roadway crossing the centerline of the roadway coming to final rest at the south curb of the eastbound travel lane. The driver of V2 was transported to a local trauma unit for his injuries. The driver of V1 was not transported, both vehicles were towed due to damage. A 38 year-old male that stated that he is in good health and is taking no medications at this time drove Vehicle 1. He stated he does not wear corrective lenses but states he can not see out of his right eye. He stated that he and his brother have been taking turns watching their store. It had a fire a few days earlier. He was on his way to it at the time of the crash. He stated he was traveling east in lane one. He also stated that the light was green and when he entered the intersection a vehicle traveling north came into the intersection hitting his vehicle at the right front side rotating his vehicle counter clockwise and pushing it into a parking lot on the north east corner of the intersection. He stated that is wear he came to final rest. A 53 year-old male drove Vehicle 2. He stated he is taking no medications at this time. He does wear corrective lenses for myopic (nearsightedness). He stated that he had just left his office for his daily rout and was traveling north in lane one. He traveled towards the intersection the light had changed to green and was green for around 10 seconds. He stated as he entered the intersection he saw the vehicle crossing in front of him. He stated he could not brake but thinks he steered to the right. He does not remember much after that he just kept trying to keep his vehicle from contacting any other vehicles. He stated his vehicle came to final rest on the south side of the eastbound roadway. The Critical Pre crash Event for V 1 was coded “this vehicle traveling crossing over (passing through) intersection”. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was coded “driver related error decision error aggressive driving behavior”. The associated factors to this vehicle were reckless driving behavior and this driver was thinking of his work place. The Critical Pre crash Event for V2 was coded “other vehicle encroachment from crossing street, across path”. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded to this vehicle. The Associated factor to this vehicle was this driver was thinking of his rout for the day. Witness #1 stated as he traveled west towards the intersection in lane one he noted a vehicle (V1) traveling in his lane towards him then a northbound vehicle (V2) contacted V1 at the right side of V1's vehicle rotating the vehicle counter clockwise and it traveled into a parking lot to final rest. The northbound vehicle (V2) rotated clockwise onto the sidewalk contacting a fire hydrant then traveling to the south side of the roadway to final rest. Witness #2 stated he was traveling west and had come to a stop in the left turn lane at a red light. He stated he was stopped for 10 to 15 seconds and saw a vehicle traveling east half in lane two and half in lane one of westbound traffic entering the intersection and being contacted by a northbound vehicle in lane one both vehicles traveled east out of his view because of the type of vehicle he was driving. This researcher after speaking with the officer found he had been following the driver of Vehicle 1 and the vehicle had ran three traffic lights and one stop sign before entering the intersection and crashing. Southbound approach curved left with an uphill +4% grade and a +4% super-elevation. This was a dark curve being that it is surrounded by tall trees that didn't let much morning light through. This crash occurred during dawn hours, 9 minutes prior to offical sunrise. It was a clear morning with light traffic and no other adverse atmospheric conditions. VI, a 1990 Ford Ranger, compact pickup with one occupant was traveling northbound in lane one. V2, a 1994 Jeep Cherokee, two door, compact sport utility with one occupant was traveling southbound in lane one. An unknown large non-contact truck was driving southbound directly in front of V2. As VI approached the T-intersection, it intended on turning left. VI slowed down to allow the large truck to pass. Once the non-contact truck cleared the intersection, VI made its left turn and contacted the left front of V2 (event 1) in the intersection. After impact VI rotated approximateley 230 degrees counterclockwise, traveled 20 meters (66 feet) and came to final rest facing east, partially in northbound lane one, partially on the grass shoulder. V2 rotated counterclockwise, left 7.3 meter (24 feet) of yaw marks and came to final rest facing east, approximately 8.3 meters (27 feet) southwest of impact area. The driver of VI, a 17 year-old male, was transported from the scene with minor injuries. He advised that he overslept and was running late for the start of his high school class. He said his truck usually takes 10 minutes and up to defrost the windows, but he didn't have time to pre-start it that morning. He got in his cold truck and headed to school. Even though he was running late, he claimed to have driven the speed limit the whole way. When he approached the T-intersection to make a left, there was a large truck coming from the opposite direction. He slowed down to 24 kph (15 mph). Once the truck passed, he made his left turn. He turned left into the left front of V2, which he never saw coming. He claims V2 didn't have its lights on, but it was light enough to where he didn't need them. His vehicle was towed with disabling damage. The Critical Pre crash Event for VI was "This vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection." The critical reason for the critical event was "Inadequate surveillance, looked but did not see." - a driver error. The driver of VI told me that he checked to make sure nobody was coming behind the large truck, but never saw V2. V2 not having its headlights on this dark dawn morning was an associated factor. This driver was in a hurry. He admitted to sleeping too late and being late for the start of high school. He had large speakers in his truck that he was listening too while driving. He felt drowsy when he started driving that portion of the trip. He takes medication daily to treat ADHD and insomnia. He reports engaging in heavy weight lifting 3 times weekly. He wasn't wearing his glasses at the time of crash; they are not required by the state for driving. Vehicle factors include, dirty window, low tread depth on all tires and over inflated tires. The driver of V2, a 55 year-old male, was transported from the scene with minor injuries. He advised that he was driving to work like he did everyday his whole life. He was driving around the left curve at 72-80 kph (45-50 mph). He noticed VI in the opposite direction wanted to turn left, but had no clue they would turn into him. As he entered the intersection, VI turned and struck his left side, causing him to spin out and become pinned in his vehicle. He claimed to have not turned his headlights on because it was light enough out where he didn't need them on to see. His vehicle was towed with disabling damage. The Critical Pre crash Event for V2 was "Other vehicle encroachment, from opposite direction, over left lane line." The critical reason for the critical event was not coded to this vehicle. An associated factor was failing to turn headlights on. Even though it was light enough to see, his dark colored vehicle made it difficult for other drivers' to see him. He made a false assumption that the driver of VI would yield the right of way to him. He reported suffering from high blood pressure and diabetes and takes prescription medication to treat the same.

The 54-year old female driver of the vehicle 2 stated she was traveling less than 40 kph (25mph) in the left southbound lane when vehicle 1 emerged from in front of the truck and crossed her travel lane. No Critical Reason was coded for the driver of vehicle 2. The Critical Precrash Event occurred when vehicle 1 turned across the path of vehicle 2. The posted speed limit in the area was 72 kph (45 mph). Roadway conditions were dry and clear with no adverse atmospheric conditions. V1 is a 1995 Ford Ranger, compact pickup with one occupant traveling westbound in lane two. V2 is a 1994 Mercedes C220, four-door intermediate with one occupant traveling westbound directly in front of V1. V1 was traveling at a higher rate of speed than V2. V1 was unsuccessful and struck the back, right plane of V2 with its front, left plane (Event 1). After impact, V1 traveled toward the left, departing the roadway into the grass median. V1 then exited the median and crossed both eastbound lanes and struck a utility pole of the south roadside with its front, center plane (Event 2). V1 then backed up and came to rest on the south roadside facing east, 85.0 meters (279 feet) from original impact point. After impact, V2 lost control in the westbound lanes and started in a slight clockwise yaw. V1 then regained control before departing the roadway to the right. V2 came to rest facing southwest 78.1 meters (256 feet) from original contact point. The driver of V1 is a 19 year-old male who was not injured in the crash. He had been awake for 23 hours since his last sleep. He had worked all day lying tile, then drove to his friend's house for a party. He had spent the night at his friend's party where he reported staying awake all night. He denied any drug or alcohol usage at that party. When the crash occurred he was on his way home. He had just got in a major argument with his friend and was going home because he was angry and upset. He wouldn't tell me what the argument was about. While he was driving on a road he rarely drives, his friend called him and they were arguing with each other on the phone. He says he was in lane two traveling 80 kph (50 mph). He came up on V2, but never saw her until she applied her brakes. He was coming up on her quickly and tried to steer right to avoid contacting her. He didn't have enough time and contacted the back of V2. He then lost control of his truck, crossed the grass median to the left, crossed two eastbound lanes and struck a utility pole with his front-end. He told police that he did not contact the utility pole, but they later figured it out due to extensive front-end damage. His vehicle was towed from the scene. He had only had one hour of sleep in a twenty-four hour period and was likely fatigued. He had just had an argument with a friend and was in an angry and upset mood while he was driving. Arguing with his friend on his cell phone while driving distracted him. Due to him being distracted by the argument on the cell phone, he failed to look far enough ahead and recognize a vehicle was in his lane of travel. He was traveling too fast for conditions, didn't realize caution was need. He was an inexperienced driver (2 years driving), driving on a roadway he rarely drives. His right, rear tire lacked safe tread depth. All the windows of his truck were very dirty, possibly contributing to him not seeing V2. *Despite all of his tires being the same age, the RR had significantly less tread depth. A possible cause could be aggressive driving behavior (squealing tires from a stop). The driver of V2 is a 60 year-old female who was not injured in the crash. She woke up early this day to go to a friend's house where they take a long walk to promote good health. She makes this trip to her friend's house daily. She was negotiating the right downhill curve between 48 kph (30 mph) and 64 kph (40 mph). Due to the steep grade, she applied her brakes in an attempt to slow her vehicle down. As he was traveling around the slight left curve at 120 kph (75 mph) in the left lane, the driver suffered a blackout, or fell asleep. The passenger noticed the car steering left toward the guardrail, then noticed the driver was not awake. He grabbed the wheel and steered right, at which time the driver woke up. The driver was disoriented and started to fight his passenger for control of the steering wheel. The passenger was larger and stronger so won the fight for the wheel. The car then veered off the road to the right, heading for the tree line. The driver was still disoriented but believes he hit his brakes prior to impact. The passenger became scared of striking the trees, screamed, unbuckled himself and attempted to jump in the back seat for protection from impact. He was in between the front and back during impact. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was “This vehicle traveling, off the edge of the road on the left side.” The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was “Sleeping, that is, actually asleep,”- a driver related error. According to family members, this driver has no history of blackouts or any other remarkable medical conditions. It's very likely he fell asleep due to the slight roadway departure angle. The driver did get 7 hours of sleep the night before the crash, but was also participating in “strenuous recreational activities,” (moving furniture, painting, renovating house). He only drives this roadway monthly, possibly an experience factor. All four tires were underinflated, a tire/wheel deficiency factor. The cloudy, overcast skies were the only weather related factor.

The driver of V1 stated that her speed would not have been fast due to the deer hazard in the area. The driver of V1 stated that she has no memory of the events leading up to the accident but was tired after a long day. Investigation revealed no avoidance maneuvers performed, neither braking nor steering. There were no skid marks leading to the point of impact, and the path of V1 was straight into the wood chipper. Inspection of V1 at the scene revealed an excessive amount of clutter within the vehicle. The Critical Pre-Crash event for V1 was "Other motor vehicle in lane, other vehicle backing." The Critical Reason for the Critical Pre-Crash Event was coded as an "Unknown recognition error." Associated factors coded to the driver of V1 include fatigue and the physical conditions of pregnancy (7 months) and a thyroid condition for which the driver took a prescription medication. A 28-year old female drove V2. Her passenger, a 32-year old male, stated that he didn't witness the event. Neither occupant was injured. The driver of V2 was traveling to her residence. Once she reached the driveway of her residence she pulled past the driveway on the opposite side of the road, activated her emergency flashing lights and then began reversing, maneuvering the truck with the attached chipper across both lanes towards the driveway. She had maneuvered the chipper across the westbound lane when she saw V1 approaching from the left (eastbound direction) and assumed V1 would yield to the emergency flashing lights. She continued to reverse, tracking the position of the chipper in her right side mirror as she backed up. The impact of V1 knocked the driver into the passenger seat. The driver stated that she was driving her boyfriend's truck and hauling the chipper from his job site and had departed the job site one and one-half hours prior to the crash. The Critical Pre-Crash Event for V2 was "This vehicle traveling over the lane line on the left side of travel lane." The Critical Reason was not coded to this vehicle. Associated factors coded to the driver of V2 include alcohol involvement, an exterior factor because the driver of V2 was watching the chipper in her side mirror, and a false assumption because the driver of V2 assumed that V1 would yield the right of way to her.

V1 was occupied by three occupants and two pet dogs in cages. According to the driver, V1 was traveling at either 50 or 55 mph. Both the vehicle and the trailer yawed in a counterclockwise direction across all three lanes while still attached to each other. The trailer jackknifed with the back of the trailer striking the left back corner of V1. The jackknife appears to have occurred at approximately the same time that V1 ran into the median concrete barrier with its front right. This could be verified due to a sharp bend in the yaw marks at the impact points. V1 came to rest facing eastbound near the median barrier with the trailer straddled across the inside shoulder and lane 3. V1 and the trailer had both been moved prior to researcher's arrival but V1 was still at the scene. No one in V1 was injured. V1 was driven by a 37-year old female. The driver said that when, for an unknown reason, the trailer started fishtailing behind her, she tried to slow down by hitting the brakes without lock-up and steering in both directions to regain control. The driver could not tell when the trailer jackknifed during the loss of control. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V1 was this vehicle lost control due to a jackknife event. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was a driver related error of traveling too fast for conditions (towing the trailer). There was an advisory warning on the trailer stating that the trailer should not be pulled at a speed exceeding 45 mph. One associated factor coded to the driver of V1 was fatigue - the driver admitted sleeping in the vehicle earlier for just a few hours during a long cross-country trip and that she was trying to stay alert by taking caffeine pills. Other associated factors were a work-related back injury that caused her discomfort and stiffness, poor directional control, this being the first time she had driven on this roadway, and not being comfortable towing the trailer in a vehicle that she had owned for less than a month.

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This crash occurred on a parking lot drive-through roadway. The roadway was an unmarked undivided 2-lane roadway with designated parking spaces on either side of the roadway. At the time of the crash, it was early afternoon and the roadway was dry. At the point of the first impact, the roadway was level. There was no posted speed limit sign. V1 was a 2005 Chevrolet Malibu that was traveling westbound on the roadway. V2, a 2002 Nissan Pathfinder, pulled out of a parking space to the left of V1, attempting to turn left to travel in the same direction as V1. The front left of V1 struck the right side front of V2. This impact rotated V2 in a counterclockwise direction, causing the left front of V2 to strike a parked vehicle to its left, a 2004 Ford Focus. After striking V2, V1 struck two other parked vehicles on the right and then continued westbound on the roadway. V1 then departed the roadway to the right and struck some bushes and trees. The police incident report narrative stated that the driver of V1 claimed that her brakes had failed; however, an inspection of V1 revealed no such defect. The police speculated that after the initial impact, the driver of V1 kept her foot on the accelerator, thinking it was the brake. The driver of V1 also told police that after the first impact, the air bag deployed and the car went out of control. It is likely that the air bag deployment confused the driver of V1 and also blocked her field of vision. The driver of V1, a 29-year old female, along with her 5-year old daughter who was a passenger in the back seat, were transported to a local hospital with minor injuries. The driver of V2, a 43-year old male, went later for treatment. There was a passenger in the parked Ford Focus who was sitting in the passenger seat doing paperwork as she waited for a relative inside one of the buildings. She reported that she looked up and saw V1 going very fast prior to the crash, and then saw V2 coming toward the vehicle that she was in. The passenger in the parked vehicle was not injured. The Critical Precrash Event for the driver of V1 was when another vehicle encroached into her lane from a driveway, turning into the same direction. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded to the driver of V1, although some associated factors were coded to her. First of all, the driver of V1 was traveling too fast for a parking lot. The passenger in the parked vehicle reported this, and the hard impact to V2 verified this. For this reason, the driver of V1 was also coded as driving aggressively. The driver of V1 was also unfamiliar with her vehicle - she told police that she had just rented the car from a rental agency. The driver of V1 had just left a doctor's office, and it was possible that she had received upsetting news or that she was late for another appointment. The driver of V1 cut her interview short, and so much information was unknown. The Critical Precrash Event for the driver of V2 was when he began to turn left onto the roadway. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was coded as inadequate surveillance, a recognition error. He told police that he never saw V1 approaching because it was going too fast. Although it was believed that the driver of V1 was traveling too fast, it was not believed that this was the reason that the driver of V2 did not see V1. It was believed that the driver of V2 failed to look to his right immediately before pulling out from his parking space. The driver of V2 reported that he took a prescription medication to lower his cholesterol, but had no other health issues. In summary, the crash occurred when V2 pulled from a parking space and attempted to turn left onto a drive-through roadway. The driver of V2 reported that he did not see V1 prior to the crash. The driver of V1 contributed to the crash by traveling too fast for the roadway/parking lot.

The pre-crash north/south roadway is straight, dry and worn concrete paving with a grade of -8% and a stop sign at the intersection. The pre-crash east/west roadway is straight and level worn concrete paving. Both roadways are non-divided two-lane roads and have posted speed limits of 48 kmph (30 mph). Vehicle one (V1), a 2004 Volvo V70 station wagon, driven by a 77 year-old female was stopped at the intersection. Vehicle two (V2), a 1983 Porsche 944 coupe, driven by a 16 year-old male with one passenger was traveling eastbound on the roadway. V1 was waved into the intersection by an uninvolved vehicle stopped across the intersection at the corresponding stop sign. V1 pulled into the intersection into the path of V2, who was traveling at a high rate of speed. V2 slammed on the brakes and skidded for 25 meters (82 feet), striking the left rear of V1. After impact V1 traveled an additional 12.45 meters (40.9 feet) to final rest for a total distance of 37.45-meters (122.87 feet). V1 was not disabled, and was driven from the scene. V2 was disabled, and was towed later in the day. None of the occupants were injured. The driver of V2 stated that he was driving the speed limit, but the rough calculation of his speed derived from the skid marks was 71 to 79 kmph (44 to 49 mph). The driver of V 2 stated that he had just shifted into 3rd gear, which is consistent with the speed mentioned and hard acceleration. When the driver of the Volvo pulled away from the stop sign, the distance to V2 would have been sufficient had V2 been traveling the speed limit. Furthermore, if V2 had not locked the brakes into a long skid, but had modulated the brakes better, the collision could have been avoided despite the speed and distance to the intersection. Inexperience and aggressive driving were factors for this driver. The driver and passenger were also having a social conversation before the crash. An additional environmental factor for both driver's for this crash was a view obstruction caused by parked vehicle's parked along the southwest corner of the intersection. The critical pre-crash event for V2 was coded: other vehicle encroachment, from crossing street- turning into opposite direction. The critical reason for the critical event not assigned to this driver. This driver was traveling too fast to be able to respond to unexpected actions of others, and driving in an aggressive manner. The critical pre-crash event for V1 was coded: this vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection. The critical reason for the critical event coded to the driver of V1 as a driver related decision factor: turned with obstructed view.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

Output Text 1:
Vehicle 1:  Decision, Driver Inexperience, Drugs, Roadway, Surveillance present problem 
Vehicle 2:  Aggressive Behavior, Distraction, Driver Inexperience, Roadway, Speed/Distance present problem

1    Case Summary The crash occurred in the intersection of a five-lane east/westbound roadway divided by a grassy median with crossovers and a two-lane north/southbound roadway. The east/westbound roadway had three westbound lanes and two eastbound lanes. The westbound lane one was marked as a right turn only lane. The right turn only lane curved right and merged with lane one of the northbound roadway. This roadway was dry, straight, level, asphalt with a posted speed limit of 72kph/45mph. The north/southbound roadway had one lane in each direction with a posted speed limit of 48kph/30mph. It was asphalt, dry, straight and level. At the intersection there were painted gores on the north/southbound lanes marking a single northbound lane from the crossover of the eastbound lanes, and a single right turn only lane from the southbound lanes to the westbound lane one. This portion of the roadway curved to the right and merged with westbound lane one, with a radius of curvature of 24m. There were no traffic control devices for either roadway. V1, a 1998 Ford Taurus, four-door with one occupant was traveling westbound in lane two. V2, a 2002 Toyota Camry, four-door with one occupant was traveling from the southbound roadway in the curved section that merged into westbound lane one. V2 stopped in its lane as it negotiated the curve to the right. V1 braked and swerved to the right and its front end struck the left side of V2. After impact V1 continued traveling about two meters until it came to rest facing northwest against the west curb. V2 rotated in a counter-clockwise direction and came to rest against the west curb about three meters southwest of impact facing east. V1, the Ford Taurus, was driven by a 56-year old male who received non-incapacitating injuries and was transported to a hospital. The driver reported that he was running errands and traveling about 64kph/40mph. He said that three or four seconds before impact he saw V2 and thought it was southbound in the northbound lane about to turn left in front of him. He advised that he braked without lockup and steered to the right in an unsuccessful attempt to avoid striking V2. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was this vehicle traveling off the edge of the road on the right side. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was driver related, false assumption of other's actions. The driver mistakenly thought V2 was traveling the wrong direction and assumed that it was preparing to turn left in front of him. As a result he unnecessarily braked and steered to the right directly into V2. This vehicle was assigned associated factors for driver physical condition (the driver had a bad hip and high blood pressure and was using several medications); driver behavior, decision, false assumption - that the other vehicle would turn in front of him; and for driver behavior, other, he chose inappropriate and unsuccessful actions. V2, the Toyota Camry, was driven by a 21-year old female who was not injured. The driver reported that she was returning to work from an errand and stopped in the curved section of road from the southbound lane before trying to enter the westbound roadway. She said she stopped (3-4 seconds) and looked to her left for westbound traffic. She said she saw V1 suddenly swerve to its right directly towards her vehicle. She advised that she did not have time to take any avoidance actions before V1 struck her and spun her around. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was other vehicle encroachment from crossing street across path. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded for this vehicle. There were no associated factors for V2.

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This single vehicle crash took place during morning hours on an east/westbound street. This street had one lane for travel in each direction, east and west, and the travel lanes were physically undivided. The posted speed limit for this street was 40 kmph (25 kph). The weather was clear, the asphalt surface was dry, and though the roadway just prior to the first impact was slightly curved and on an uphill incline, the roadway was generally level and free of any defect. Vehicle 1 (V1), a 1994 Toyota Land Cruiser driven by a 32-year-old female, was traveling westbound in lane 1. The main witness to this crash was an individual who just happened to be driving behind V1. According to this witness he stated that he was very aware of V1's presence, but that V1 was not doing anything out of the ordinary. The witness stated that the driver of V1 was not driving aggressively or speeding in anyway, and was maintaining her lane very well. After both vehicles negotiated two uphill curves and were approaching a very straight and level portion of the roadway, the witness explained that it looked as if V1 was about to make a left turn onto a side-street (though there was no turn-signal activated) when all of a sudden, it seemed as if she changed her mind and attempted to steer back to the right. According to the witness, it was at this point that V1 just "hit the gas". (verbatim) V1 accelerated and instead of returning to her travel lane V1 struck the southwest curb of an intersection with her front right tire and departed the roadway. This was the first impact of twenty-three. The witness continued to drive along in his travel lane as he watched V1 travel the entire length of one block downing residential railings, breaking concrete steps, and then when V1 reached the end of the block it literally drove over a stop sign pole, bending it to the ground. V1 continued across the street and entered a second residential block where it continued to cause the same type of damage as it had caused on the previous block. Additionally on this block, V1 struck 2-parked vehicles causing disabling damage to both. At the end of this second block, V1 struck a raised concrete wall and came to its final rest. According to this witness and other neighbors who were standing outside at the time of the crash, the driver quickly exited her vehicle and began to pray in a language that they did not recognize. A medic unit arrived and took the driver to an ER for evaluation. The witness who had been driving behind V1 stated that he was certain that she must have had a heart attack or some other physical emergency based on the way she accelerated and traveled out of control for as long as she did without attempting to brake, steer, or return to the roadway. He stated that he was shocked to see that the driver was fully conscious and apparently healthy. A second witness, who did not see the first harmful, had just returned home from dropping her grandchild off at daycare, parked her vehicle, and stepped onto the curb. She stated, "From what I seen ... she came up here (witness pointed to the front yards and sidewalks), she hit the railing and I looked up and I seen the car coming down and it was over my head and it comes down, and I start screaming for someone to help me ... cause I thought it was going to land on me." (verbatim) This witness was so close to V1 that hot fluid from the vehicle splattered across her exposed foot as V1 passed by her. (witness was wearing sandals) This witness also explained that V1 then smashed into her vehicle that she had just parked, as well as the vehicle parked behind hers, and then V1 struck the wall and came to its final rest. The distance between where V1 first struck the curb and the wall where it came to its final rest was 160 meters. (528 feet) While the researcher was surveying the extensive crash scene, the driver of V1 returned to her vehicle from the emergency room. Two relatives were escorting her. When the driver was asked what had happened, the driver stated that it was all up to God. When asked if she had attempted to try to take control of her vehicle once she left the roadway she said no and that she had panicked. A relative of hers then said something to her in French Creole and the driver of V1 was unwilling to say anything else. Many attempts were made to re-contact this driver including a visit to her residence. The driver did not return phone calls, respond to a mailed letter, or answer her door. The driver had been celebrating his best friend's wedding the night before the crash. After the reception he went back to the groom's house for a party. The driver hadn't slept in 20 hours prior to the crash. At the wedding he danced and consumed alcohol, which contributed to his fatigue. Based on scene evidence, it was not believed that the driver fell asleep or passed out at the wheel. Speed was also a factor in the crash. A speed reconstruction indicated that the vehicle was traveling at approximately 90-100 mph prior to the crash sequence. The driver was charged with DWI, and the result of the BAC test as per the medical records was .174. The driver also tested positive for cocaine and cannabinoids.

1    Case Summary Case 2007-156 The crash occurred in the crossover four-way intersection of three roads. The main roadway was a north/southbound road divided by a grass median. North of the intersection there were three southbound lanes and two northbound lanes. South of the intersection there were three northbound lanes and two southbound lanes. East and west of the intersection there were two two-lane undivided roads with one lane in each direction. East of the intersection to road curved to the left with a radius of curvature of 41m. West of the intersection the road curved to the right with a radius of curvature of 58m. The roads were all dry, asphalt and level. The posted speed limits were 89kmph/55mph for north/southbound traffic and 48kmph/30mph for east/westbound traffic. There were no traffic controls for north/southbound traffic. There were stop signs for east and westbound traffic and there was a yield sign in the median crossover for westbound traffic. The crash happened on a weekday afternoon while traffic was light under clear skies. V1, a 2000 Ford Explorer four-door SUV with one occupant was westbound in lane one. V2, a 1996 Chevy Corsica four-door with one occupant was southbound in lane one. V3, a 1993 Jeep Cherokee four-door SUV with two occupants was stopped eastbound in lane one. V1 travelled across the northbound lanes, the median and was traveling westbound across the southbound lanes and the path of V2, when its right side was struck by the front of V2 (Event 1), which braked without lockup prior to impact. The impact caused V1 to roll longitudinally one-quarter turn onto its left side (Event 2) as it rotated in a clockwise direction and skidded toward V3. The top left back of V1 struck the front left corner of V3 (Event 3), which took no evasive action. V1 came to rest on its left side facing northwest about 9m southwest of the first impact. V2 had already been moved but according to police came to rest facing west just west of the impact point. V3 had already left the scene prior to researcher arrival. V1 and V2 were both towed from the scene. V1, the Ford Explorer, was driven by a 30-year old female who was not injured. The driver had left the scene prior to researcher arrival and refused to return numerous phone calls and messages left by the researcher and a letter hand-delivered to her home my the researcher. According to the driver of V3, who had a clear and unobstructed view of both V1 and V2's approaches, V1 was traveling eastbound and did not stop at the stop sign east of the intersection or at the yield sign in the median before she started to cross the southbound lanes and the driver did not appear to take any evasive action prior to impact. She added that V1 was on it's left side when its top struck V3. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was this vehicle traveling crossing through the intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was driver related, unknown recognition error, due to the fact that the driver would not grant an interview. Associated factors were assigned to V1 for the same reason. V2, the Chevy Corsica, was driven by a 40-year old male who was not injured. The driver reported that he was traveling at the speed limit in lane one while returning home from a friend's house. He said he did not see V1 crossing from his left to right in front of him until just a second before his vehicle struck hers He added that he did brake in an unsuccessful attempt to avoid impact. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was other vehicle encroachment from crossing street across path. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded for this vehicle. An associated factor was assigned to V2 for driver behavior, recognition, inadequate surveillance - failure to look far enough ahead. V3, the Jeep Cherokee, was driven by a 27-year old female who was not injured. The driver reported that she had just left the store behind her and had stopped at the eastbound stop sign and was waiting to enter the crossover and turn left as she returned to her home. The driver of V3 said she had a clear and unobstructed view of both V1 and V2's approaches, She added that V1 was traveling eastbound and did not stop at the stop sign east of the intersection or at the yield sign in the median before she started to cross the southbound lanes and the path of V2. V1 skidded eastward sideways for about 76m, where it's rear end traveled off the edge of the shoulder onto the grass roadside and the rear end struck a 30cm tree trunk (Event 4). That impact caused V1 to rotate in a clockwise direction while it continued traveling east. V1 came to rest on the grass roadside facing east approximately 84m east of the first impact point. After Event 3, both V2 and V3 veered to the left off the edge of the road onto the grass median. V2 came to rest on the median facing east approximately 140m east of the first impact. V3 came to rest on the median facing east approximately 152m east of the first impact. All three vehicles were towed due to damage. V1, the Chevy Beretta, was driven by a 60-year old male, who was coded for incapacitating injuries and transported to a hospital. The 35-year old right front seat passenger was up and arguing with police and EMT's at the scene. He was transported to the hospital where he expired later as a result of his injuries. The driver refused to answer any researcher questions on the advise of counsel. According to the police report both occupants were under the influence of intoxicants. There was a partially consumed half-gallon bottle of whiskey visible on the right front floorboard. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was "other motor vehicle in lane traveling the same direction with a lower steady rate of speed". The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was driver related, "type of driver error unknown" due to the driver's refusal to be interviewed. Associated factors assigned to V1 were for driver physical condition due to the observed presence of alcohol and the police reported BAC (test results unknown); and for driver behavior, decision, traveling too fast for conditions reason unknown. Police follow-up report to researcher indicated that driver's BAC was .21 and passenger's BAC was .13. V2, the Chevy Impala, was driven by a 68-year old female. Both she and her 12-year old right front seat passenger were coded for incapacitating injuries and transported to a hospital. The driver later reported that she was returning home traveling at an unknown speed. Her vehicle's EDR indicated that she was traveling 79 kmph (49 mph), with no braking, at impact. She said she never saw either V1 or V3 and took no evasive actions. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was "other motor vehicle in lane traveling the same direction at a higher rate of speed". The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded for this vehicle. Associated factors were assigned to V2 for driver, physical condition due to the driver's bad back, previous aneurism, and bad heart and the numerous medications she was taking; and for driver behavior, recognition - she was conversing with her passenger. V3, the GMC Acadia, was driven by a 42-year old female. Neither she nor her occupants were injured. The driver reported that she was traveling about 89 kmph (55mph) while returning home from a trip to the zoo. She said she took no evasive actions because she never saw either V1 or V2 until after V2 struck hers. The Critical Precrash Event and the Critical Reason for the Critical Event were not coded for V3 because it was not involved in the first harmful event. There were no associated factors assigned to V3.

On the north side of the intersection, there were six lanes of travel, three in each direction. On the north side of the intersection, there were no dedicated parking lanes. The roadway was straight, its vertical profile was level, and the bituminous surface was dry and free from any noticeable defects. Vehicle one (V1), a 1999 Ford E350 utility box truck, was traveling northbound in the first lane of travel. Also traveling northbound and behind V1 was Vehicle two (V2), a 2002 Chevrolet Geo Prism. After V1 and V2 cleared the intersection, V1 attempted to change lanes into lane one, striking V2 and forcing V2 partially off the roadway to the right. V2 was disabled and sitting partially off of the roadway and partially on the east sidewalk. V1 pulled just ahead of V2's final rest and parked in lane one, a travel lane. There were two witnesses to the crash. V1 was driven by a 42-year-old male who had one occupant with him, a co-worker and friend. During a complete on-scene interview, the driver stated that he had been working on the morning of the crash and that he and his co-worker had just left a local eatery to have lunch. He explained that he pulled out of the parking lot of the restaurant and made a right into lane one heading north, and began to approach the intersection. He stated that he was very familiar with this street and that after he passed through the intersection he was going to make his way into the new rightmost travel lane because he intended to make a right at the end of the next block. He stated that after he passed through the intersection he applied his right turn signal and checked his right side-view mirrors. Driver of V1 stated that he checked his side-view mirrors twice, saw that he was clear and made his lane change to the right. At this point the right side of V1 struck the left side of V2 and forced it partially onto the right curb. Driver acknowledged that he was very full from lunch and had been talking to his passenger about the move to a new home that he and his family were about to begin. He stated that he and his family were excited about moving to a new home but also acknowledged that it was somewhat stressful. V2 was driven by a 56-year-old female and was carrying one adult passenger. Upon researcher arrival, V2's driver and occupant had already been transported to an emergency room due to injuries of unknown severity. The attorney for this driver did phone the researcher and he stated that the driver of V1 had "admitted fault" and that V1 driver had acknowledged that he had failed to use his turn signal and that he had failed to look in his side-view mirror as he had reported on the day of the crash. 1    Case Summary The crash occurred on a north/south two-lane, urban, level residential roadway on a weekday evening shortly before midnight. This arterial road was straight and then made a slight 15-degree curve to the left before continuing straight again. There was a two-lane side street that intersected the roadway from the west in the curve. A curb and sidewalk were present on the west side of the road. Conditions were dark and dry and the posted speed limit was 64 kmph (40mph). Vehicle 1 was a 2003 Chevrolet Express 2500 series cargo van traveling south on the two-lane road. The driver was traveling too fast to negotiate the curve and lost control. He crossed over into the northbound lane in a slight yaw and departed the left (east) side of the road. The van struck a utility pole with its front left corner and continued south 8m before running over a large rock that was 40cm high with its right front wheel. The right front wheel was sheared off and the left front wheel dug into the soft ground as the van rode up on the rock. Vehicle 1 became airborne and began to roll to the left as it rotated clockwise. The van then suffered its most severe impact when its top back struck a large tree after rolling one-quarter turn. Damage to the tree, which was 30m from roll initiation, extended from the ground 2.8m up the tree. The vehicle came to rest on its top facing west 5m off the road and 54m south of initial impact with the pole. Data was extracted from the EDR and indicated that there was a non-deployment event 0.3 seconds prior to the airbag deployment event and that there were one or more associated events not recorded. Both the non-deployment and deployment pre-crash graphs were identical and indicated that five seconds prior to algorithm enable the vehicle was traveling 94mph (151kph) with brake switch circuit status on and throttle percentage at 0%. Three seconds prior to AE the throttle was applied at 74% and brakes were released with a speed of 88mph (142kph). At two seconds the brakes and throttle were both off and the speed was down to 80mph (129kph). One second prior to AE the throttle was at 0% the brakes were on with a speed of 71mph (114kph). Maximum SDM recorded longitudinal velocity change was 5mph and the driver was unbuckled. The 20-year old male driver was able to crawl out of the vehicle through a broken window and fled the scene. Police located him at his intended destination ½ mile from the crash scene when a friend requested an ambulance a short time later. He was transported to an area trauma center where he was hospitalized. The driver refused a blood alcohol test but told researchers the night of the crash he'd drank about eight beers and taken four Xanax "totem poles." He had worked his regular eight-hour day after a normal night's rest and had gone to a bar after work. Although he was very familiar with the road, he was in a company van that he rarely drove when the crash happened. Police charged the driver with several violations, including DUI. The Critical Precrash Event for the driver of Vehicle 1 was when he lost control of his vehicle due to traveling too fast for conditions. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was coded as “too fast for curve/turn,” a decision error. Excess speed was coded as an associated factor, as well as alcohol mixed with a prescription medication that had the possible side effects of drowsiness. This medication had not been prescribed to the driver of Vehicle 1. Due to the excess speed of Vehicle 1, aggressive driving was also coded as an associated factor. In summary, the crash occurred when the driver of Vehicle 1 attempted to take a minor curve in the road at a speed more that twice the posted speed limit and lost control of his vehicle. Associated factors included alcohol and drug usage, as well as unfamiliarity with the vehicle.

The main road ran north/south with an intersecting east/west roadway. Southbound approach was straight with a 3 percent downhill profile. There were two straight lanes and a left turn lane. A grass median divided this north/south roadway. Eastbound approach was straight with a 9.5 percent downhill profile. There was one right turn lane, one straight/left turn lane, and one westbound lane. A double-yellow line divided this roadway. The posted speed for north/south traffic was 72 kmph (45 mph), while east/west traffic was posted at 56 kmph (35 mph). The crash occurred during afternoon hours on a weekday. Conditions were daylight, dry and clear. Vehicle 1 was a 2003 Mercury Grand Marquis four-door sedan with two occupants traveling southbound in lane two. Vehicle 2 was a 2003 Dodge Durango large utility vehicle with one occupant traveling eastbound in lane two. V1 and V2 entered the intersection at the same time and collided at a right angle. The front of V1 contacted the left front side of V2 (event 1). After impact V1 deflected toward the left and V2 deflected toward the right. The right side of V1 side slapped the left rear of V2 (event 2). V2 then continued on a right path and came to final rest on the southbound west shoulder facing south. V2 left 22.2 meters (72.8 feet) of left front tire impressions on the ground, post impact. After event 2, V1 departed the roadway to the left onto the grass median that divided north/south traffic. V1 traveled 36 meters (118 feet) before striking a concrete drainage crate with its undercarriage (event 3). V1 continued another 25 meters (82 feet) before coming to final rest facing south in the grass median. The driver of V1 was a 75 year-old female who was transported from the scene with evident injuries. She and her husband had left their home, made a quick stop by their insurance company office for 20 minutes and were en-route to a local tow yard to pick up personal belongings from their other vehicle that they crashed the night before. The husband had let his wife who suffers from memory loss and dementia drive because he received a DWI the day before and his license had been taken away from him. They headed southbound in lane two at 48 kmph (30 mph) toward downtown on a roadway she rarely drives. She claimed to have never seen V2 pull out from the right, but the husband did see V2 and was yelling at her to slow down because they were going to collide. She was unable to recall the post crash events, but the husband said they went across the grass median to the left and struck a concrete drain crate that stuck up 6-7 inches before coming to final rest. Their vehicle was towed from the scene with disabling front-end damage. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V1 was "Other vehicle encroachment, from crossing street, turning into opposite direction." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded to this driver. This driver was on a straight path when V2 interrupted its travel. This driver suffers from diabetes for which she takes prescription medication. She also suffers from memory loss and dementia, which explained her not being able to answer many of the interview questions 9 days post crash. She was talking to her husband, who was in the front seat, prior to the crash. She also participated in recreational activities five days per week, including swimming and aerobics. Her vehicle's tires lacked adequate tread depth. She also had an opaque homemade cardboard sun visor taped to the top third of her windshield to help cut down on sun glare. This vision obstruction possibly blocked her view of the overhead traffic signals. Other associated factors coded to this driver include inadequate surveillance and an illegal maneuver for failing to stop for the traffic signal. The driver of V2 was a 49 year-old female who was transported from the scene with evident injuries. According to police, as she approached the intersection, she intended on turning left to travel northbound. The driver of V2 hired an attorney who refused to allow his client to grant an interview until the claim was settled. Her vehicle suffered disabling left side damage and was towed from the scene. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V2 was "This vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Type of driver error unknown" due to the lack of interview. From the medical report, it was learned that the driver suffers from hypothyroidism for which she takes prescription medications. No other associated factors could be coded to this driver.

V1 then overcorrected by steering hard to the right, causing the vehicle to rotate and yaw clockwise, leaving 31 meters (102 feet) in yaw marks. V1 then departed the roadway to the right still in a clockwise yaw, left side leading, and contacted a small shrub (event 1) with the right front. V1 then hit a 1.4-meter x 1.5-meter brick well house (event 2) with its left front, causing brick to scatter and strike two parked vehicles. V1 then went on to contact 2 small shrubs (event 3 & 4), with its right side. V1's tires then tripped on a bituminous driveway, causing it to roll one-quarter turn. V1 traveled approximately 7 meters on its left side before contacting a 90-cm diameter tree with its top. V1 rotated counterclockwise approximately 145 degrees before coming to final rest facing northwest on its wheels. A 22-year-old male, who was a full-time student at home for a break, drove V1. For an unknown reason, the driver of V1 and his best friend were driving northbound that morning. A witness saw the driver of V1 stop in the roadway and pick up the passenger. He then stopped at a stop sign, squealed tires and proceeded straight at a high rate of speed. Another witness reports the driver coming past her at a high rate of speed. As she looked in her rear view mirror she saw V1 depart the roadway to the right, strike a brick well house and roll over before contacting a tree. No other information was available due to the driver and passenger both having serious head injuries and surrogates not being able to provide much detailed information about the driver. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was "this vehicle traveling off the edge of the road on the right side." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was a decision error- "too fast for curve." V1 was traveling well above the posted speed limit for the roadway. It was believed that the speed of V1 ultimately led to the control loss and caused it to run off the right side of the roadway and strike the tree. It is not known why the driver was driving above the speed limit. His parents said that he worked nights on the opposite side of town and they didn't know why he might be going in that direction that time of day. Associated factors coded to the driver of V1 were aggressive driving and overcompensation.

Vehicles are allowed to parallel park but not between the hours of 1500 and 1900 hours. The crash occurred in the early afternoon prior to those hours. The speed limit was 56 kmph (35 mph) in both directions. A two-way left turn lane separates northbound and southbound traffic, and parking lanes border the first lanes of both directions. At the time of the crash it was raining, and the roadway was in poor condition with large cracks disrupting the surface. Vehicle one (V1), a 1999 Chevrolet Metro four-door sedan, was traveling northbound in the second lane, abreast of a non-contact vehicle in the third lane. Vehicle two (V2), a Hino Motors straight truck with a 2003 air-compressor trailer attached, was parked against the east curb, ahead of V1. The non-contact vehicle began to enter the second lane, and V1 veered right to avoid it. V1 entered the parking lane, where the front of V1 impacted the left side of the air-compressor trailer. V1 rotated clockwise from impact and rolled two quarter-turns towards its left side, coming to rest in the second northbound lane on its top. The 51-year-old male driver of V1 did not provide an interview. He stated to police that he veered right to avoid the non-contact vehicle and immediately impacted the trailer. Inspection revealed that all of V1's tires were significantly under-inflated. The critical pre-crash event for V1 was: this vehicle traveling, over the lane line on the right side. The critical reason for the event was attributed to V1, and was coded: Driver related performance error, overcompensation. The driver was arrested for theft and unlawful taking of a motor vehicle. The primary cause of the crash was listed on the police report as DUI on the part of the driver of V1. Contributing factor was speed greater than reasonable or prudent. The air bags in V1 did not deploy in this frontal impact. Vehicle history shows the vehicle had 5 owners. The year prior to the crash, a salvage title/certificate was issued@ 124,787 kilometers (77,539 miles). Less than a month later, a frame inspection was performed. The Crash Data Retrieval System data was downloaded and analyzed. It was apparent the data retrieved was not from this subject crash. It insinuates the crash recorder was never replaced after the last crash and therefore the airbags if installed in the vehicle at the time of this crash could not have deployed.
