1    Case Summary This two-vehicle fatality collision occurred during daylight hours, shortly after noon on a weekday. The collision occurred on a five-lane, two-way, dry, level, bituminous roadway, consisting of two eastbound lanes, two westbound lanes, and a center two-way left-turn lane. A bike lane and a parking lane border the north and south edges of the roadway. The collision occurred near a T-intersection to the north, which is controlled by a stop sign for southbound traffic. The east/west roadway is designated a 64 kmph (40 mph) zone. Vehicle one (V1), a 1996 Isuzu Rodeo four-door SUV, was traveling eastbound in the first lane. Vehicle two (V2), a 1974 Chevrolet Corvette coupe, was traveling westbound in the second lane of opposing traffic. A non-contact vehicle abreast of V1 moved right across the lane line, partially entering V1's lane. V1 veered right to avoid the non-contact vehicle, then corrected left to avoid departing the road. V1 lost traction and began to rotate counter-clockwise as it moved across the eastbound lanes and the left turn lane. As V1 entered the second westbound lane, the front of V1 impacted the left side of V2. Both vehicles moved northeast and rotated counter-clockwise. V1 rolled three quarter-turns towards its right side, coming to rest near the northeast corner of the T-intersection. V2 came to rest near V1 and caught fire. Both vehicles were towed due to damage. The driver of V1 is an unlicensed 28-year-old female, who could not be located for an interview. The driver stated to the police that a non-contact vehicle, to the left of her, partially encroached into her lane without signaling a lane change. She swerved right into the bike lane order to avoid a collision, then swerved back into her lane and lost control. The witness interview agrees with her statement. Police reported a speed for V1 of 90 kmph, or 56 mph based on the pre-impact skid distance of its yaw mark. The driver of V2 is a 49-year-old male, who sustained fatal injuries as a result of the collision. The autopsy found marijuana in the driver's body, but had negative results for alcohol. A surrogate for the driver could not be located. The Critical Pre-Crash Event for V1 was coded: This Vehicle Traveling - Over the Lane Line on the Left Side of Travel. The Critical Reason was assigned to V1 as a driver related performance factor: overcompensation. This driver avoided the non-contact vehicle by steering to the right into the bike lane; however, she made then overcompensated by steering left at a high rate of speed and initiated control loss of the vehicle. The Critical Pre-Crash Event for V2 was coded: Other Vehicle Encroachment - From Opposite Direction Over the Left Lane Line. The Critical Reason was not assigned to the driver of V2.

The driver advised that he was traveling at about 105 kmph (65 mph) when he started to decelerate because he noticed traffic was slowing in his lane. Then traffic came to an abrupt halt. The driver said that he stopped and then looked in his rear view mirror to see if the Acura was going to be able to stop in time. It was apparent that the other vehicle was going to make contact, so he braced himself for impact. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was other motor vehicle in lane, traveling in same direction with higher speed. The Critical Reason for the Event was not coded to this vehicle. This driver had no associated factors, and was not thought to have contributed to the crash.

V1 was driven by a 22-year-old male driver that works at a warehouse Monday thru Friday 07:45 - 17:00 hours. His normal sleep schedule is anywhere from 23:00 - 24:00 hours to 07:00 hours and he usually feels rested when he wakes. He admitted that he had a late start this morning and was still a little tired when he left his house for an unknown reason. While he was not involved in an argument the past 12 hours he acknowledged that he frequently argues with his mother about his future plans. He left his home this morning and drove north toward his job. After driving less than 2 miles from his home, he looked down inside his car to either grab a cigarette or change the CD (he is not certain which action he took.) Once he looked up at the road again, he saw the traffic in his lane stopped. He immediately braked and steered right causing him to depart the roadway and drive into a ditch where his vehicle rolled onto its roof. He said he unbuckled his seat belt and exited the car through the passenger door. He reported that he was not injured in the crash and refused EMS treatment, but there were visible scratches on the top of his left hand. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was coded: (23) this vehicle traveling off the edge of the road on the right side and the Critical Reason for the Critical Precrash event was coded: (111) as a recognition error; internal distraction because of the other non-driving activities mentioned above. Associated factors, coded to this driver, include being in a hurry since he mentioned he had a late start to the day and he had to travel 35 miles in 20 minutes to get to work on time and false assumption for believing that traffic would continue without stopping.

V1 then traveled up the embankment contacting two small pines trees within 3 meters (10 feet) of one another (event 2 and 3). V1 then came to final rest on its top facing south after rolling six-quarter turns a total distance of 17 meters (56 feet). The driver of V1 is an 18 year-old male driver who suffered abrasions to his body, but refused treatment from EMS on-scene. The front of v1 struck a cement wall which ran parallel to the roadway 12 meters from the shoulder. V1 rotated counterclockwise and the right side of v1 struck the wall as v1 continued traveling westward. 1    Case Summary The two-vehicle crash happened in a signal-controlled four-legged intersection with pedestrian crosswalks. Southbound approach had five lanes, two of them being dedicated left turn lanes. It was straight with a 3.5% uphill profile. Eastbound approach had six lanes, three left turn lanes, two straight lanes and a right turn only lane. This road had a curve right profile with a 2.6% downhill grade. Both roadway were divided by a raised concrete medians and contained curbs and sidewalks. The posted speed limit for all directions was 72 kph (45 mph) and both had bituminous asphalt. Setting was morning hours on a weekday. Conditions were dawn hours, cloudy and dry. Vehicle 1 (V1) was a 2001 Dodge Ram 3500 Large Van with one occupant traveling southbound in lane two. Vehicle 2 (V2) was a 1999 Ford Mustang, two door, with two occupants traveling eastbound in lane four, the far right left turn lane. VI and V2 entered the intersection at the same time. The front of VI contacted the left side of V2 (event 1). VI claimed to have braked, but there was no evidence on the roadway. After impact, VI steered left while V2 went toward the right. The right side of VI contacted the left side of V2 (event 2). VI left 10 meters (33 feet) of visible right front tire marks before coming to final rest facing east. V2 traveled laterally toward the south leaving 4.8 meters (16 feet) of right front yaw mark before rotating clockwise and coming to final rest facing south, appoximately 12 meters (40 feet) south of first impact. The driver of VI is a 52 year-old male who was not injuries in the crash. He is self-employed as a package delivery driver who works 45 hours per week, varied based on client's needs. He had just come from his warehouse where he loaded his van with 600lbs of packages. He was en route to a nearby town 100 miles away to deliver the packages. He had driven four miles when the crash occurred. He reported driving straight ahead and looking straight ahead while traveling in the center lane at 64 kph (40 mph). He said he knows he had a green light and denied any internal or external distractions. As he entered the intersection, he noticed V2 pulling out in front of him from the right. He applied the brakes, claiming the ABS engaged, but never felt any pulsating in the pedal. He also thinks he steered left in an attempt to avoid a collision. His vehicle was not towed due to disabling damage. The Critical Pre crash Event for VI was "Other vehicle encroachment, from crossing street, turning into opposite direction." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Not coded to this vehicle." V2 pulled into the path of VI, therefore causing VI to collide into V2. The driver of VI was inattentive to his driving task, but his thought focus is unknown. He also failed to look far enough ahead and see that V2 was proceeding into the intersection. He is far-sighted, but not required by law to wear glasses while driving. The skies were cloudy at the collision. The driver of V2 is a 20 year-old who was transported from the scene with perceived serious injuries. Him and his brother had just left their house and were on their way to work when the crash occurred. That was the only information available about this driver provided by a third brother who showed up on-scene. A language barrier caused me to be unable to arrange an interview. The police officer on scene said that V2 was stopped at the light before it turned green. The driver had to be extricated from the vehicle. His vehicle was towed from the scene with disabling left side plane damage. The Critical Pre crash Event for V2 was "This vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Unknown." We know this driver entered the intersection, where he was struck by VI. We don't know whether or not he saw VI. The cloudy skies are the only known associated factor for this driver.

Although the guardrail extended down nearly the entire west (right) side of the road, there was a small gap in the guardrail of approximately 2 meters and the vehicle struck the south end of the guardrail in the gap. The Explorer then climbed over the guardrail and rolled left two quarter turns down a 45% tree and brush covered embankment, striking a large tree with its right as it rolled. It came to rest facing northwest on its top 20 meters southwest of initial impact. The SUV was towed due to damage. Vehicle #1 was driven by a 20-year old male that was not belted with no deployed airbags. He was partially ejected in the crash and dead at the scene. The time of the crash is unknown; a passer by noticed the vehicle down the embankment during morning rush hour. Although blood alcohol results were not available prior to case release from the PSU the driver was videoed at a local nightclub obviously intoxicated and vomiting in the early morning hours before the crash. The Critical Precrash Event for Vehicle #1 was this vehicle traveling off the edge of the road on the right side. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was overcompensation. Associated factors included inappropriate/unsuccessful avoidance action and poor directional control. Other associated factors are unknown since it was a single occupant fatality.

Westbound approach curves left with a 7% super elevation, then straightens out as it crosses over a river. There are adequate breakdown lanes on both sides of the roadway as well as rumble strips. There is a metal guardrail that begins 51 meter (167 foot) prior to this roadway crossing over the river. Along the bridge there are positive concrete barriers present. The posted speed limit is 104 kph (65 mph). Setting was afternoon hours on a weekday. Conditions were cloudy with heavy rain coming down at the time of crash. V1 is a 2003 Chrysler Town & Country, minivan with one occupant traveling westbound in lane three. V1 was traveling the posted speed limit around the left curve with a high super-elevation. As V1 exited the left curve, it hydroplaned and started to rotate counterclockwise before departed the roadway to the left. V1 then contacted the metal guardrail with its front plane (Event 1), 11.2 meters (37 feet) prior to the concrete bridge barrier beginning. After V1 struck the guardrail, it defected and continued to rotate counterclockwise. V1 re-entered the travel lanes, as it was rotating counterclockwise. V1 traveled 86.2 meters (283 feet) after impact before coming to final rest on the left shoulder inches away from the concrete barrier facing southeast. V1 suffered disabling front-end damage and was towed from the scene to an unknown location, where it could not be located for inspection. The driver of V1 is a 38 year-old male who was not injured in this crash. He had been on the road driving for 40 minutes prior to the crash. He was on his way back to work from a business meeting 40 miles away. He says it was raining hard as he came around the left curve at 104 kph (65 mph) in lane three. All of a sudden his vehicle hydroplaned and traveled toward the left side of the road. He departed the road, struck the metal guardrail with his front-end, then spun around one complete time before coming to final rest on the shoulder. He says as he was spinning in the travel lanes he thought he might have struck another vehicle but really was in shock and couldn't recall the exact events. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 is “this vehicle traveling, off the edge of the road on the left side.” The Critical Reason for the Critical Event is “Traveling too fast for conditions: too fast for the heavy rain conditions.” This driver stated it was raining heavily prior to the crash, but yet he still decided to drive the posted speed limit, causing his vehicle to lose control. This driver told me he was over-worked and under-paid by his employer, coded as work related stress. He suffers from depression in which he takes a popular prescription anti-depressant. The heavy rain and wet roads were both coded as roadway factors. The vehicle was not inspected, so it is unknown if there were any tire of mechanical deficiencies.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

At the location of the crash, there is a westbound/eastbound city street intersecting with a northbound/southbound 5-lane thoroughfare. The northbound/southbound road has a 72 kmph (45 mph) speed limit and does not have a traffic signal or sign. V1, a 2003 GMC Safari with one occupant, was traveling west and proceeded to pass through the intersection. V2, a 1997 Ford F-250 with one occupant, was traveling south toward the intersection. He said he never saw the truck before he entered the intersection because there was a real estate sign at the intersection obstructing his view. V2 was driven by a 23-year-old male driver. For this trip today, he was traveling from one job site to another. He reportedly was traveling south at 72 kmph (45 mph) approaching the intersecting roadway when a van from his left pulled out in front of him. This driver stated that he never saw the van (V1) approach the intersection but admittedly was not looking at the side street for traffic. He said he locked-up his brakes once he saw the van in his lane but could not stop in time to avoid the crash. He did not suffer any injuries in the crash and went back to work after his employer arrived on the scene. Vehicle #2, a 1992 Dodge Daytona, was westbound with no grade, but a 9% negative super elevation to the north (right) 20 meters prior to impact. V2 started sliding straight and came over into my lane and hit us. No airbags in the vehicle deployed. The Critical Precrash Event for Vehicle #2 was this vehicle traveling over lane line on left side of travel lane. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was too fast for conditions. V1 was not towed from the scene but V2 was towed due to damage. V1 was driven by a 38-year old female that worked part-time for a catering business. She reported that she was in good health but had an unknown vision condition. On the morning of the crash, the driver of V2 drove her children to school. She was returning home from school and was driving on the same roads she always travels. As she drove west on the 2-lane undivided roadway, she claimed the Mitsubishi (V2) in front of her suddenly stopped without warning and she struck the back of it. This driver reported that she braked before the impact but that V2 never indicated it was stopping or turning left, and there was not enough time to avoid the collision. This driver's vehicle was not towed from the scene and she was not injured in the crash. V2 was driven by a 33-year old female who worked as an office manager. Her typical work schedule was 8:00 AM to 5:30 PM Monday through Friday and her sleep timetable was normally 10:30 PM to 5:30 AM. She exercised every weekend with her son and did so the weekend before the crash and the day before the crash. She was near-sighted and wore contact lenses. At the time of the crash, the driver of V2 was on her way to work. Once she reached the entrance to her company parking lot, she stopped in lane 1 of the 2-lane undivided road, activated her left-turn signal, and waited for traffic to clear. She reported there was an unusually light amount of traffic for that time of day, but a car traveling in the opposite direction (southeast) caused her to wait before she could turn left. As she waited to turn, she heard what sounded "like a train" traveling behind her so she glanced at her rear view mirror and saw a large Expedition "barreling" toward her. She could not see the driver of the SUV but remembers seeing a large grille approach her. She knew the SUV would not be able to stop in time so she braced her hands on the steering wheel, anticipating an impact. The front of the SUV struck the back of her Mitsubishi, forcing her sedan forward almost 30 meters and to the eastbound roadside. She was removed from her car by EMS and was treated and released from a trauma center. She suffered a strained neck and back from the whiplash of the collision and two black eyes after contacting her sun visor. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V1 was when another vehicle was stopped in its lane. The Critical Reason for the Pre-crash Event was coded as "too fast to be able to respond to unexpected actions of others." Other than excess speed, no additional associated factors were coded to the driver of V1. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V2 was when another vehicle was in its lane, traveling in the same direction with higher speed. The Critical Reason for the Pre-crash Event was not coded to the driver of V2 and no associated factors were coded to her. A crash history report on V1's driver revealed that she had been involved in a crash 20 months earlier and was cited for failure to reduce speed. A crash history report on V2's driver revealed that she had been involved in three crashes in the past year.

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This two-vehicle crash occurred in a three-legged intersection. The main road runs north/south with in intersecting road coming in from the west. North/south traffic has no control devices, while the eastbound roadway is controlled by a passive stop sign, with no stop line present. Southbound approach is straight with a +1.6% grade and has a right turn lane that begins 83.7 meters (275 feet) before the intersection. Eastbound approach is straight with a -3.6% grade. There are sidewalks present on the west side of the roadway and none on the east side. The speed limit for both roadways is 56 kph (35 mph) and both roadways are bituminous asphalt. Setting was morning hours on a weekend. Conditions were daylight, clear and dry. Traffic was light at the time. Vehicle 1 is a 2004 Ford Crown Victoria, full size police car with one occupant traveling southbound in lane two. Vehicle 2 is a 2002 Jeep Grand Cherokee, compact utility with one occupant traveling eastbound in lane one. V1 and V2 met in the intersection at the same time and collided at an angle. The front of V1 contacted the left side of V2 (event 1). V1 then rotated counterclockwise, while V2 rotated clockwise causing the right side of V1 to contact the left side of V2 (event 2). V1 steered left in an attempt to avoid the crash, while the driver of V2 made no avoidance maneuvers. V1 and V2 came to final rest in the northbound lanes against each other. V1 was facing southeast while V2 was facing east approximately 5.5 meters (18 feet) east of impact area. The driver of V1 is a 42 year-old female who was transported from the scene with evident injuries. She is a police officer who was on her way this morning to meet several other co-workers for breakfast at a local restaurant. A co-worker on scene told me he had been on the Nextel two-way radio with her seconds before the crash occurred. He advised that after impact she was unable to find her radio to call for help, so she called him on the Nextel and advised what her location was. Her fellow officer also told me that they have extensive driver training and that she drives that roadway daily because it was her patrol area. He had to leave the scene before I could question him further. The driver was not available for an interview due to the on-going criminal case. Her vehicle was towed from the scene with disabling front-end damage. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was "Other Vehicle Encroachment, from crossing street, turning into opposite direction." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Not Coded to this driver." The driver was possibly distracted by her Nextel two-way call to her co-worker. She also had a police laptop mounted in the passenger's side of the vehicle, which was in use facing her direction, possibly causing a distraction. No other associated factors are known for this driver. The driver of V2 is a 19 year-old female who was transported by police to the hospital for treatment. She denied using any medications or drugs prior to the crash. She is a full-time college student who works as a lifeguard when not in school. She told me that she spent the night at a friend's house. She went to bed very late, and woke up very early due to being uncomfortable and unable to sleep. She reported approximately 3.5 hours of sleep. She got in her car to head home down a road she had only driven on twice. She can't recall too much after that except that she ran the stop sign, which she wanted to turn left at and hit another car. She never saw the other car coming prior to impact. After impact, she hit her head "really hard" on the steering wheel. Her vehicle was towed from the scene with disabling damage. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 is "This vehicle traveling, turning left at the intersection." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Inattention"- a driver related error. The driver told me that she was inattentive to her driving, but was unable to offer a reason. She did report listening to the radio, a possible distraction. She was impaired by alcohol at the time of the crash, impairing her ability to drive safely. She reported being drowsy when she started driving , and with only 3.5 hours of sleep, it is likely that she was fatigued. She was also not familiar with this roadway. She had only driven it twice. She most likely failed to look right or left prior to entering the intersection, which was coded as an associated surveillance error. She does take daily acne medication which has dizziness listed as a side effect.
Name: Case Summary, dtype: object

The road was level and curved slightly to the right and had a posted speed limit of 113kmh (70mph). At the time of the crash it was raining hard and the well-traveled bituminous road was wet. V1, a 2003 Chevrolet Malibu driven by a 33 year-old female, was traveling at approximately 60-65 mph in lane two. The driver realized that the speed was too fast for the wet conditions and was intending to change lanes to the right, slower lane. V1 began to hydroplane as non-contact vehicles in lane one and lane three passed v1 at the same time. The driver of v1 felt her vehicle lose contact with the pavement and begin to swerve to the left, then to the right as driver steered right, and then back to the left again. V1 left the center lane and rotated counterclockwise across lane three and the left shoulder until the back right of v1 struck the guardrail. The right front of v1 struck the guardrail and the driver then steered left to avoid contact on the right passenger area where her 12 year-old niece was the front right passenger. V1 rotated clockwise and came to rest 75 meters down the road in lane three facing east. The fully restrained driver and passenger of v1 were transported to a local trauma center. V1 was towed from the scene due to damage. The Critical Precrash Event coded for v1 was 'this vehicle control loss due to poor road conditions-puddle/water on road. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was coded as 'decision error-too fast for conditions'. An Associated Factor was coded for the driver's prescribed hormone medication which has a possible side effect of drowsiness.

1    Case Summary This two-vehicle collision occurred during mid-afternoon hours in a construction area, on a busy undivided highway. The pre-crash roadway is normally a five-lane roadway, but at the time of the crash the number one lane in both directions was closed off by construction barrels so there was one through lane in each direction and a 2-way center turn lane. The pre-crash roadway has a slight grade, and is straight with bituminous paving. The weather was clear and the roadway was dry at the time of the crash, but according to the V 1 driver, it was just starting to rain very lightly. The roadway has a posted speed limit of 72 kmph (45 mph) and there was an advisory speed limit of 40 kmph (25 mph) posted in the construction area. Vehicle one (V1), a 1999 Ford Flatbed pick-up track had been stopped behind a "large panel truck" facing northbound in the center turn lane, and entered the oncoming lane intending to turn left. The V1 driver, a 38 year old male, stated that he had pulled approximately 1.2-meters (4 feet) into the oncoming lane when he noticed vehicle two (V2) and he then stopped. V2 did not stop and the two vehicles collided front to front. The impact moved V1 slightly counterclockwise and it came to rest near the point of impact. The V1 driver stated that after noticing V2, he had sufficient time to back up into the center turn lane, but did not do so because he had poor visibility behind him, and believed that V2 had plenty of time to stop. V2, a 2003 Hyundai Accent, was traveling southbound in lane one. The V2 driver, a 42 year old male, stated he saw V1 pull out and pause, then proceed forward into his path. He stated that he then hit his brakes and then attempted to steer to the right, but was unable to avoid the crash and the two vehicles collided front to front. Both vehicles were towed from the scene. The critical pre-crash event for V1 was coded: this vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection. The critical reason for the critical event was assigned to the driver of V1 as a driver decision error: false assumption of other's actions. After the driver realized that he had encroached into V2's travel lane he falsely assumed the other driver would make avoidance actions for the collision. He stated that he had time to take such actions himself but made an assumption the other driver would make them. Another associative factor for the collision the the sight line was partially blocked to V2 by the large panel truck in front of him. The driver stated that initially he couldn't see beyond truck in front of him. Additional associative factors: The V1 driver stated that he takes Zoloft for anxiety, and has sleep apnea with current CPAP treatment, however he was well rested and was not in a hurry. He also stated that he is paid on an hourly rate, and was not in a hurry, as the company policy is "safety first". The critical pre-crash event for V2 was coded: other vehicle encroachment; from opposite direction- over left lane line. The critical reason was not coded to this vehicle. Possible associative factors for V2 include unfamiliarity with the road, and the V2 front tires had very low tread. A possible associative factor for both vehicles is the presence of the orange cones and barrels on the lane line; they are at eye level and the road is effectively without a shoulder area.

The speed limit was 40 kmph (25 mph) and there were no adverse atmospheric conditions. Vehicle one, a 2005 Ford Mustang, was traveling westbound. The driver, who stated that he was traveling 40-48 kph (25-30 mph), attempted to turn right into the alley and lost control, traveling off the right side of the roadway. The front of the vehicle struck a pole that was located just west of the alley entrance. The driver left the scene, leaving the vehicle partially in the roadway and partially on the curb. According to the police officer, a few neighbors pushed the vehicle from final rest into a street parking space. The vehicle was towed due to damage. The driver, a sixteen-year-old male, was unlicensed and had never received any driver training. His father had rented this vehicle. During the week that his father had possession of this vehicle, the driver had driven this vehicle once prior to the crash. He had never driven on the roadway where he had the crash. He was well rested but concerned about the vehicle overheating. He stated that the traction control was turned off causing him to drive off the road when he abruptly attempted to turn right in to the alley. The critical pre-crash event for this vehicle was coded as "this vehicle traveling; off the edge of the road on the right side." The critical reason for the critical pre-crash event for this vehicle was coded as "driver related factor; poor directional control (failing to control vehicle with ordinary skill)."

It was late afternoon just prior to dusk on a weekday and had begun to snow shortly before the crash, lightly covering the road. The posted speed limit on the highway was 113km/h (70mph). Vehicle 1, a 1994 Isuzu short bed pickup truck driven by a 21 year old male, was traveling east in lane one at approximately 89km/h (55mph) when the back of his truck began to fishtail on the snowy road as v1 came in contact with an icy area. The driver pumped the brakes and attempted to steer left and right to control the vehicle. The back of the truck swung to the left and V1 left the roadway on the right side with the left side leading. V1 traveled laterally in a clockwise rotation down an embankment until the tires dug in and tripped V1 into a rollover as the embankment then began to slope upward. V1 rolled 4 quarter turns with the left side leading and then rolled back 1 quarter turn onto the driver's side as V1 swung around facing back toward the highway and came to final rest. The fully restrained uninjured driver of V1 exited the vehicle through the open backlight area. V1 was towed from the scene due to damage. The Critical Precrash Event coded for V1 was “this vehicle control loss due to traveling too fast for conditions”. The snow at the time of the crash had not been falling long enough for the driver of V1 (or most others in the area) to adjust his speed accordingly. The cold pavement, particularly under the overpass, was conducive to creating slippery, icy patches. The driver's stated travel speed was below the posted limit but that was related to evening rush hour traffic. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was coded as “Environmental/Highway related-slick roads (low friction road surface due to ice”. A driver decision error was coded for “traveling too fast for conditions-did not realize caution was required” due to the fact that the driver did not recognize the need to slow down as soon as it began to snow. Although the driver had only been driving the truck full time for a few months, the crash occurred at the end of a record setting winter season of snowfall, possibly indicating more of a decision error than inexperience. One possible Contributing Factor for this crash was the 6 x 50# sandbags located in the bed of the compact pick-up truck. The driver put them in to add weight and they were put in lined up in 2 rows of three from wheel well to wheel well right in the middle of the bed. The driver described the rear of the truck as spinning out as soon as he hit the icy patch, and then fishtailing/wagging back and forth. A possible Associated Factor was noted for frequency of driving the road, as the driver stated that he was driving that route home after his first day of work at a new job, but one he held 5 years prior. Although he was familiar with the road, he had not driven on it in the recent past. However the roadway environment was similar to most of the highways in the area. The driver of v1 was wearing contact lenses to correct a myopic condition, but indicated no other health related conditions. The driver has a valid driver's license with no endorsements, and was in compliance with his corrective lenses restriction. The driver tried to back off the tracks but was unable too before being contacted on the right front side of the vehicle by the train. The vehicle then spun around and hit the train again with its right rear. It then rolled over 3 quarter turns before coming to final rest on the driver's side of the vehicle. The driver of vehicle #1 a 46-year old female was transported to the hospital with injuries. The driver stated that she was on her way to pick up her paycheck from her employer at the time of the crash. As she started up the grade she stated she had not noted the signal lights flashing or the bells ringing. She stated that after hearing the bells she looked to her right and saw the train was to close to get out of the trains path. After being contacted by the train and rolling to final rest she was able to climb out through the passenger window and walk over to the curb were she waited for the police and EMS to arrive. The vehicle was towed due to damage. The Critical Precrash Event for vehicle #1 was coded other, (specify) Crossing over railroad grade. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event for V1 was inadequate surveillance (failed to look, looked but did not see). An associated factor to this driver was she was taking a medication to help her fall to sleep, some possible side effects are drowsiness, dizziness and daytime tiredness, slowness of movement. She also stated her concern for a daughter's baby shower.

Vehicle 1 was a 2000 Chevrolet Venture minivan that was traveling south in the first lane of travel. Vehicle 2 was a 2006 Honda Accord coupe that was traveling west in the first lane of travel. Both vehicles were approaching the intersection at approximately the same time. V2 had the green light. V1 was driven by a 24-year old male who was driving his brother home. As he neared the intersection, he looked down and to his right to pick up a juice bottle that had fallen over. He heard his brother say, "Oh, no," and he first looked at his brother and then at the intersection. At the last second, he saw V2 crossing in front of him, from left to right. He had no time to react and the front of V1 struck the right side of V2. V2 was driven by a 28-year old female. As she approached the intersection, she noted V1 approaching from the north but thought it would stop. Upon entering the intersection, V2 was struck by V1. After impact, V2 rotated counterclockwise and skidded to the southeast curb of the southbound lanes. V1 came to rest in the second southbound lane. Both drivers and the occupant of V1 were checked out by the EMS unit on scene and released. Both vehicles were towed due to damage. The driver of V1 reported that he was in good health and was not taking any medication. He does not wear corrective lenses. He admitted that he was driving with a suspended license. He stated that he was going to drop off his brother at their mother's home before the crash occurred. He said that by the time he saw V2 it was too late to take any avoidance maneuvers. The driver of V2 reported that she was in good health and not taking any medications. She does not wear corrective lenses. She stated that she was on her way home from school. As she approached the intersection, she had noted the southbound vehicle and thought it would stop. She had no time to take any avoidance maneuvers. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V1 was coded: this vehicle crossing over (passing through) intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Pre-crash Event was coded: internal distraction. Associated factors coded to this driver include conversation with the passenger, inadequate surveillance for not looking at the traffic signal, an illegal maneuver, and excess speed. The EDR indicated that within 5 seconds of the crash, V1's travel speed was 37 mph in a 25 mph zone. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V2 was coded: other vehicle encroachment from crossing street, across path. The Critical Reason was not coded to this vehicle. One associated factor coded to this driver was a false assumption of other road user's actions, because the driver of V2 did see V1 approaching the intersection, but assumed that it would stop. The witness stated that he was walking north on the east sidewalk on the north side of the intersection. As he heard heavy braking he looked to his left to see V1 entering the intersection on a red light. He saw V1 strike the right side of V2. He said that he called 911 and then checked to see that everybody was alright.

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This single vehicle rollover crash occurred along a busy stretch of a four lane interstate. This road was straight with a slight uphill grade. A positive concrete barrier divided this roadway from opposing traffic. Both sides of the road contained rumble strips and the pavement was fresh, new bituminous with a posted speed limit of 96 kph (60 mph). Setting was afternoon hours on a weekday. Conditions were clear, sunny and dry. Vehicle 1 was a 2002 Ford Explorer Sport, two-door, compact utility, with two occupants traveling westbound in lane one. VI had just merged onto the interstate and wanted to switch lanes to the left. As VI started to switch lanes to the left it noticed a vehicle in its lane, so it swerved back to the right. VI swerved too hard to the right and left its travel lane and onto the right paved shoulder. It then over corrected to the left to return to its original travel lane. Once back in its travel lane, it over corrected a third time to the right, causing it to rotate clockwise. VI left 30.2 meters (99 feet) of yaw marks before rolling over on the paved shoulder with the left side leading. VI then started to tip over and contacted the metal guardrail with its top. VI then traveled on its roof 10 meters (33 feet) and came to rest on its roof partially on the roadway and partially on the guardrail, facing east. The driver of VI, a 28 year-old, pregnant female, had evident injuries and was transported to the trauma center for treatment. She reports being gestational diabetic from her pregnancy and checks her blood sugar four times daily. An hour prior to the crash, she reported her blood sugar to be high. Her and her husband were on their way to get something to eat on the side of town where the husband's parents live. When they merged onto the expressway, the driver wanted to switch lanes to the left. She checked her rearview mirror and saw no cars, so she then checked her blind spot and saw no cars. As she began to change lanes, she noticed a car at her left front corner, which she thinks must have been in lane three and just merged right. To avoid colliding with this car, she swerved right, but too hard and went onto the shoulder. She then over steered left in a panic to return to the roadway. She then swerved right causing her to rotate clockwise. Her and her husband can't recall the rollover events except they think it overturned two times. Her vehicle was towed from the scene with heavy top damage. The Critical Pre crash Event for VI was "This vehicle traveling, off the edge of the road on the right side." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was "Overcompensation"-a driver related error. This driver failed to make a simple maneuver to keep her vehicle in control. She unnecessarily pulled her wheel to hard to the right and left, therefore causing her to lose control and overturn. Her pregnancy and diabetes were both health related factors. She did a good job of checking traffic behind her before switching lanes, but she failed to check traffic to the left front, left side of her. She also reported talking to her husband socially prior to the events unfolding. She admitted that the reason for her overcompensation was that she panicked, when she thought she was going to hit the car in her lane to the left.

The Pre-event movement of V1 was accelerating in traffic lane, the Critical pre-crash event was turning left at intersection and the Critical reason for critical pre-crash event was inadequate surveillance (looked but did not see). The vehicle seemed to be in good mechanical condition when inspected though scene evidence revealed skid marks from only 1 tire. This could be attributed to the gravel surface of the roadway. The impact caused V2 to yaw counterclockwise, leaving 15.2 meters (50 feet) of yaw marks before contacting the northeast curb with its RR tire. The impact with the curb caused V2 to overturn four-quarter turns (10.0 meters/ 33 feet) about its right axis and come to final rest on its wheel facing west along the sidewalk. V1 came to final rest just east of impact point with disabling front-end damage facing east. The driver of V1 is a 22 year-old male who was not injured. He had overslept on this day and was late for work. He left home within minutes of waking up. He works part-time and was awoken by a co-worker's telephone call asking where he was. He says he was in the left turn lane, stopped for 5-10 seconds before he got a green arrow. He started turning left, then glimpsed right and noticed V2 was coming toward him quickly from the opposite direction. He claims to have hesitated before entering the intersection, but then braked when he realized V2 wasn't stopping. He did apply the brakes, but still contacted the left side of V2. He claims that V2 must have run the red light. He did have his radio up loud prior to impact, but claimed no other distractions. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 is “This vehicle traveling, turning left at intersection.” The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was “Inadequate surveillance, failed to look far enough ahead prior to turning,”-a driver error. He had overslept and was in a hurry to get to work because he was late. This driver suffered from ADD as a teenager as well as a collapsed lung, both physical factors. This driver was inattentive to his driving task, he claimed to be listening to his radio very loudly as well as being concerned and worried about being so late for work. I believe he also falsely assumed that V2 would yield the right of way to him because he thought he has a green arrow. He only drives this roadway weekly and police told me that they've never seen the left turn arrow change to green; they say it's always a solid green and unprotected. The driver of V2 is an 18 year-old female who was transported from the scene with a serious leg injury. Her boyfriend/passenger is the owner of a pressure washing business. She helps him run the company and also works in the field with him. On this morning, they were on their way to a job site. They had just left their house a few minutes minutes prior. They were driving straight in lane three at 72 kph (45 mph). Prior to entering the intersection, they saw V1 in the left turn lane with their blinker on, but never thought V1 would turn left in front of them. By the time she realized they were getting struck, there were no reasonable avoidance maneuvers to take. V1 contacted their left side causing the vehicle to yaw and rotate counterclockwise. She says they struck a curb and overturned several times before coming to rest on their wheels. She said all the cargo in rear of the van shifted during the rollover, including the 250-gallon water tank. She thinks the water tank contributed to them rolling over as many times as they did due to its weight. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 is “Other vehicle encroachment, from opposite direction, over the left lane line.” The Critical Reason was “Not coded to this vehicle.” She denied using any using any medications (legal or illegal), but medical records indicated recent cocaine, THC, and opiate usage. She was talking to her boyfriend prior to the crash occurring, a non-driving activity. She did experience work related stress due to lack of work (cold weather, drought, weak economy). She did have these concerns on her mind while driving that morning. The LF tire was overinflated, a tire/wheel deficiency. The 250-pound water tanks in the back as well as the pressure washers were not properly secured, causing them to shift during rollover sequence (inadequate securement).

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary This two-vehicle crash took place in the early afternoon on a weekend in the middle of a 4-way intersection. The weather was clear, the asphalt surface was dry, and the roadway was level. This particular roadway had a somewhat atypical layout in that lanes 1 and 4 were designated for motor vehicle travel through the intersection, while lanes 2 and 3 were designated lanes for use by both the trolleys that travel on this north/south roadway as well as for motor vehicles intending to make left turns. Lanes 1 and 2 ran northbound and lanes 3 and 4 ran southbound. A 30-meter raised concrete median separated lanes 1 and 2 and lanes 3 and 4, and served as a physical barrier providing safety for individuals to both board and exit the trolley service. Overhead red/yellow/green traffic lights controlled the movement of north and southbound travel as well as an overhead sign above lanes 2 and 3 that read "Trolley" and "Left Turn." Red/yellow/green traffic control devices were also present on each corner of the intersection. Poorly visible (faded) left turn arrows were also painted on the asphalt surface of lanes 2 and 3. There was also one lane for residential parking on each side of this street. The posted speed limit for this roadway was 48 kmph (30 mph). V2, a 2006 Dodge Durango, was traveling northbound in lane 2, the left turn lane. V1, a 2006 Chrysler Pacifica, had just exited the northbound shoulder where it had been parked and entered lane 1, also traveling northbound. The driver of V1, a 34-year old female, explained that she had been visiting friends and had been parked in the right shoulder lane. Her intention was to approach the intersection and make a left turn from the right lane because she was unable to access the left turn lane due to the presence of the raised concrete barrier. The driver of V1 entered the intersection and proceeded to make the left turn across lane 2 when the front of V2 struck V1's left door. The PAR estimated the travel speeds of both vehicles as being approximately 30 mph (48 kmph). The PAR for this case also stated that the driver of V2 was intending to make a left turn as well, although even without an interview from this driver, the evidence strongly suggests that V2 was in fact proceeding straight through the intersection instead of making the required left hand turn. The driver of V1 said that she was certain that V2 was going straight, but even stronger evidence for this conclusion can be found in both the extent of damage to V1's left side and the damage patterns and the positions of both vehicles at their final resting positions. Observation on scene revealed that it is a common occurrence for drivers in lane 2 to go straight through the intersection. This may be due to the faded nature of the painted arrows; however, extended observation leads one to conclude that most drivers simply choose to ignore the lane markings. The driver of V2, a 25-year old male, could not be located for an interview. He told police that he was intending to make a left turn at the intersection. Both drivers were transported from the scene directly to a hospital with (unknown) minor injuries. Both vehicles were towed. The Critical Pre-Crash Event coded to V1 was "This Vehicle Traveling/Turning Left at Intersection." The Critical Reason for Critical Pre-Crash Event was coded as "Illegal Maneuver." Associated factors coded to the driver of V1 include a false assumption in that she assumed that traffic in lane 2 would in fact turn left, and a roadway-related factor in that the lane markings were faded and not clearly visible. The Critical Pre-Crash Event coded to V2 was "Other Vehicle Encroachment/From Adjacent Lane (same direction) - over right lane line." The Critical Reason was not coded to V2; however, it was believed that V2 was proceeding straight through the intersection rather than turning left. This was coded as an associated factor (an "other" decision error). Another associated factor coded to V2 was a roadway-related factor in that the lane markings were faded and not clearly visible. No other factors could be coded to this driver due to the lack of an interview.

Vehicle three (V3), a 1994 four-door Mercury Grand Marquis LS, was parked on the west side of the southbound roadway, to the right of V2. The critical reason for the critical pre-crash event for V2 was not coded to this vehicle. V3 was not involved in the first harmful event. Once V2 entered the intersection it was struck on its left front plane by the front right corner of V1 (event 1). In an attempt to avoid a collision with V2, the driver of V1 had locked up his brakes, leaving 24.5 meters (80 feet) of skid marks prior to impact and steered to the left. The right rear of V1 then side slapped the left rear of V2 (event 2). V1 continued on toward the left and came to final rest 23.4 meters (77 feet) southwest of the impact area on the southwest corner of the intersection. After impact, the force of the knee bolster airbag caused the driver of V2's right foot to hit the accelerator and travel 44.8 meters (147 feet) from the impact area. V2 traveled 180 degrees clockwise, ran over the westbound north curb, then went down a negative 13 percent slope and came to final rest inches away from a townhouse. The distance from the curb to the townhouse was 12.5 meters (41 feet). The driver of V1, a 72-year old male, suffered minor injuries but refused transport. He reported that everyday his wife picks up their grandchildren from school and brings them back to their house for the afternoon. That afternoon she was doing the same. As she approached the stop sign, she wanted to go straight across. She looked left and never saw V1 coming, but claimed that the curve makes it hard to see traffic from the left. V1 was thrown across the roadway and into an adjacent parking lot on the north side of the roadway. When V1 was forced into the parking lot it contacted V3, which was legally parked in the lot. The Critical Reason for the Critical Precrash Event was "environmental related factor, view obstructed by other vehicle." When V1 proceeded into the intersection there was another vehicle in lane one of the eastbound lanes which was masking V2. When asked about visual obstructions the driver stated that she did not recall a vehicle in the lane blocking her view. She did however receive a concussion that left her unconscious for several minutes after the crash, and is not clear about all of the events of the crash. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was "other vehicle encroachment, from crossing street, turning into opposite direction." The Critical Reason for the Critical Precrash Event was not coded to this vehicle. The driver of V2 stated that there was an SUV that was in lane one preparing to turn right at the intersection, and this vehicle obstructed V1's view of V2. It should be noted that the driver of V2 was driving on a suspended license. The driver of V2 had little experience driving this vehicle. V3 was not involved in the first harmful event. The speed limit on the arterial was 72kph/45mph. The northeast corner of the intersection had a landscaped area consisting of low shrubs and flowers in mulch with a brick and block wall labeling the subdivision. Conditions at the time of the crash were daylight and dry in mid afternoon on a weekday. V1 was a 1995 Ford Explorer traveling north on the arterial in the right lane when V2, a 2003 Hyundai Sonata that was traveling south in the left turn only lane proceeded to turn left in front of the Explorer (V1). V1 then swerved right and according to the driver of V1 also briefly touched the brakes before hitting the right side of V2 with the front of V1. V1 then veered right (east) and left the roadway and struck the wall on the northeast corner with its front, coming to rest at the remains of the wall. V2 spun over 180 degrees counter clockwise coming to rest about eight meters north of impact facing west in the 2 right northbound lanes. Both vehicles were towed from the scene. V1, was driven by a twenty-six year old female who was belted with deployed airbags and was transported due to possible injuries for treatment to a local hospital. Her three passengers were all children in child safety seats in the second row and were all also transported under the same circumstances. The driver of V1 was on her way to meet a friend for dinner. The critical precrash event for V1 was other vehicle encroachment over the left lane line. The critical reason was not coded to this vehicle. The driver of V1 was taking several medications at the time of the crash. The only other contributing factor coded to this vehicle was a false assumption that the other driver would yield the right of way. V2, was driven by a twenty-seven year old female who was belted with deployed airbags. She was also transported to a local hospital for treatment although the police called her injuries minor. The critical precrash event for V2 was this vehicle traveling, turning left at an intersection. The critical reason was coded as misjudgment of gap or others' speed. She was also on several medications, which were sourced from a medical record as no interview was obtained. The windshield of V2 was also dirty enough to obscure the drivers' view.

Case Summary:
1    Case Summary The crash occurred on a two-lane east/west roadway with no shoulders. The road makes a series of curves as it winds through a rural area. This particular crash took place in the westbound lanes near the apex of a long left curve with a radius of 351.5 meters. Conditions were daylight and sunny on a weekend afternoon. The posted speed limit was 48kph (30mph) on the level road and there was a curve warning sign with an advisory speed of 32kph (20mph). Vehicle #1 was a 1993 Honda Accord traveling westbound. As the vehicle negotiated a curve to the left the vehicle's right wheels edged off the right (north) side of the road. The driver braked and steered left, overcompensating and going into the eastbound lane. The driver then steered right in an attempt to regain control and began a clockwise rotation. After leaving 20m of yaw marks, Vehicle 1 departed the right (north) side of the road and struck a curve warning sign and a tree as it rolled two quarter turns left-side-leading to the left into a ditch. The vehicle came to rest facing south 4m off the north side of the road on its top. The drop off from the road to the ditch has a -57% grade initially and then decreases to -33% at final rest. The Accord (V1) was driven by a 20-year-old female college student who was belted in the crash. She had to be extricated by rescue personnel but refused medical transport. The police assigned her 'B' (non-incapacitating) injuries on their report. In her initial statement to police at the scene she stated that she reached over to the right to get some fast food she had just purchased out of the bag. She said she didn't take her eyes off the road, but must have pulled the steering wheel slightly to the right as she reached over which caused her to initially edge off the right side of the road. At the time of the crash the driver was on her way home from church, she was familiar with the vehicle and drove the road monthly. The Critical Pre-crash Event for Vehicle #1 was this vehicle traveling off edge of the road on the right side. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was a recognition error, internal distraction. Associated factors that contributed to the crash included non-driving activities, retrieving object from floor and/or seat; and inadequate evasive action, other-overcompensation. The researcher felt that had she not overcompensated when the vehicle edged off the road the driver could have regained control without crashing. V1 was also traveling too fast for conditions - the speed limit was 48Kph (30mph) with an advisory sign recommending 32Kph (20mph). The driver said that she tried steering to bring the vehicle under control and that she did not think she applied her brakes, but she was uncertain about that. Associated factors were assigned to this vehicle for driver physical condition - the driver had a bad knee, high blood pressure, thyroid problems and was taking multiple medications; another factor was coded for driver behavior, performance, poor directional control; and unknown for vehicle factors due to the driver's claim of mechanical problems.

The intersection consisted of a 2-lane north/south roadway intersected by a 2-lane east/west roadway controlled by properly functioning traffic signals. Both streets were bituminous, dry, straight, and level with posted speed limits of 40 kmph (25 mph). The traffic flow was normal and there were no adverse atmospheric conditions at the time of the crash. V1, a 1998 International Harvester serving as a sanitation removal vehicle with one occupant, was northbound approaching the intersection where it intended to go straight. V2, a 1995 Chevrolet Corsica with one occupant, was eastbound in lane one approaching the same intersection where it also intended to go straight. The traffic signal turned red for traffic in the northbound direction, but V1 failed to stop and entered the intersection. The driver of V1 braked hard but its front left corner struck the right side of V2. The force of the impact caused V2 to rotate counter-clockwise approximately 160 degrees before departing the roadway off the northwest corner of the intersection. V2 struck a wooden utility pole located in the northwest corner and came to final rest against it. After the initial impact V1, for unknown reasons, accelerated and left the crash scene. V1 was stopped by law enforcement less than one mile away from the crash scene. V2 was towed from the scene due to damage. The driver of V1, a 48-year old male, had been driving a sanitation vehicle regularly for 10 years. He explained that he had almost finished a day's work and had dropped off his work crew to go run an errand and that he was on his way back to pick up his crew when the crash occurred. V1 driver stated that his attention was focused on something inside of the vehicle though he would not disclose what it was stating that he felt uncomfortable discussing that topic. V1 driver freely acknowledged that he had made a mistake and when he looked up to see that his light was red he immediately applied his foot brakes and engaged his "Jake brake" and slightly steered to the left. His tires locked and he skidded into the intersection. He stated that there was nothing that he could do to avoid striking V2. V1 driver also stated that he had been diagnosed with sleep apnea and suffers from high blood pressure though neither of these conditions affected his ability to drive. V1 driver declined to discuss why he fled the crash scene but did state that he was facing undisclosed charges and is no longer allowed to work as a driver. The driver of V2, a 52-year-old male, was being loaded into a medic unit upon researcher arrival. He was traveling to visit a friend when he was involved in the crash. He stated that he was traveling east in lane one with a green light but could not see the approaching sanitation truck he did not have a chance to take any avoidance maneuvers. The next thing he remembered was being taken out of his vehicle by the medics. He was unaware that his air bag deployed or that he had struck a pole until his son informed him about the condition of his vehicle. The Critical Precrash Event for V2 was coded other vehicle encroachment, from crossing street, across path. The Critical Reason was not coded to this vehicle. The only associated factor coded to V2 was a sightline restriction as the buildings that lined the street prevented the driver of V2 from seeing V1 until it was too late. A witness to this crash had just parked her car on the east side of the north/south roadway and stepped out of her vehicle. She explained that she heard V1 racing up the street from behind her and it then passed her just to her left. She watched V1 and remembered wondering why this huge truck was speeding down the street. She then she saw that the light was red for V1. She stated that she only saw V2 for a split second because V1 was blocking her view of V2. She watched as V1 went through the red light and then V1 accelerated and left the crash scene. Immediately after the impact she stated that she saw V2 crash into the utility pole.

V2 was traveling westbound approaching the intersection in lane 2 of four lanes, with a >2% negative grade, and a posted speed limit of 56kmh (35mph). The traffic signal was green for both directions. V1, a 1989 Pontiac Grand Prix driven by a 47 year- old male, turned left in front of V2. V2, a 1993 Saturn SL2 driven by a 21 year-old female, was going straight through the intersection. The driver of V1 had no avoidance maneuvers. The driver of v2 braked without lock-up and steered right. The front of V2 struck the right side of V1. Both vehicles rotated clockwise and came to rest facing east and were towed from the scene due to damage. The unrestrained right front adult female passenger, and the two unrestrained children in the rear seat of v1 were transported from the scene due to injuries. The Critical Precrash Event coded to the driver of V1 was 'this vehicle traveling turning left at intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was 'Driver related factor-inadequate surveillance (failed to look far enough ahead before turning left). The driver of V1 was cited for 'Failure to yield right of way” and for driving with 'Unrestrained child passengers.' A Contributing Factor of strenuous non-work activities was coded for this driver due to the fact that he was moving and had loaded his possessions into his vehicle for 1.5 hours prior to the crash. The driver is overweight and has high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and diabetes which affects his foot requiring him to wear an orthopedic boot which was on at the time of the crash. Another Contributing Factor was coded for possible side effects for his daily medications for his health issues, which include insulin and unspecified blood pressure and cholesterol medications. The Critical Precrash Event coded to the driver of V2 was 'Other vehicle encroachment from opposite direction - over left lane line' and the critical reason for the critical event was coded 'Critical reason not coded to this vehicle'.

The bituminous roadway was dry and level but curved sharply to the left (as one traveled east). The traffic flow was normal and there were no adverse atmospheric conditions at the time of the crash. V1, a 2000 Buick Century, was eastbound approaching the curve. As V1 entered the curve, it failed to slow down and properly negotiate it. V1 drove off the right side of the roadway and struck a 21 cm diameter tree with its right side. V1 came to rest facing in a northeasterly direction on the roadside. Vehicle 1 was towed due to damage. V1 was driven by a belted 35-year-old female who was transported, treated and hospitalized. A family member was interviewed and provided the following information. The driver of V1 suffered from bi-polar disorder, which was treated with several medications. The driver claimed that she had blacked out due to her accidentally taking too much medication and overdosing. She had no recollection of the crash events. She had been coming from a friend's house less than a mile from the crash and drove the roadway several times per month. She had an irregular sleep pattern and usually woke up feeling drowsy. She did not work. The Critical Precrash Event for V1 was coded this vehicle traveling off the edge of the road on the right side. The Critical Reason for the Critical Precrash Event was a driver related factor, too fast for curve or turn. This was chosen because the driver stated to the first person on the scene that she was going too fast for the turn. She stated later to the police that she had blacked out. Associated factors coded to this driver were the use of prescription medications. She was taking anticonvulsants and antipsychotics, which had possible side effects of nausea, dizziness, fatigue, and drowsiness. It is believed she was also under various forms of mental and emotional stress at the time of the crash.

He decided to change lanes before he got into the intersection, so that he could go around the bus. He said that his light was green, and that the minivan had the red light. He said that just as he was about to enter the intersection (still in the process of changing lanes), a vehicle turned in front of him, with V2 following right behind. He believed that the driver of V2 saw the vehicle in front of her go, and didn't pay attention to the light. There were no independent witnesses to the crash, and both drivers had conflicting accounts of the crash events. An analysis of the damage to both vehicles revealed that V2 was traveling at a higher rate of speed than V1, and that V1 had very little speed as it entered the intersection. Due to the low travel speed of V1 and the higher speed of V2, it was believed that the driver of V2 went through a red light. It was also thought that the driver of V2 was partially distracted by the bus up ahead. The Critical Pre-crash Event for the driver of V1 was when she began to turn left at the intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded to the driver of V1. The passenger did reveal that the driver of V1 suffered from both diabetes and high blood pressure. The Critical Pre-crash Event for the driver of V2 was when he began to cross over (pass through) the intersection. The Critical Reason for the Critical Pre-crash Event was an illegal maneuver. The experience factor was also taken into consideration because the driver was new to the area, unfamiliar with that roadway and had only been driving in the United States for two years. Vehicle 2 was driven by a 63-year old belted male who did not have airbags available in his vehicle. He was transported to a local hospital with non-incapacitating injuries. The Critical Pre-Crash Event for Vehicle 2 was other vehicle encroachment from opposite direction over left lane line. The northbound roadway was one way with two traffic lanes with legal metered parking on both sides. The east/west roadway was two-way and undivided with one lane in each direction. The northbound approach was positive 3 percent uphill. The westbound approach was positive 2 percent uphill. The authorized speed limit for all directions was 56 kmph (35 mph). Conditions were daylight and dry with intermediate cloudy skies. The setting was late afternoon hours on a weekday. Vehicle 1 was a 2006 Mazda 3 compact four-door sedan with two occupants traveling westbound in lane one. Vehicle two was a 2002 Dodge Intrepid four-door full-size sedan with one occupant traveling northbound in lane one. Both vehicles entered the intersection at the same time and collided at a right angle. The left front of V1 contacted the right front of V2. Both vehicles deflected after impact and came to rest side-by-side facing northwest. V2 came to final rest in-between northbound lane one and two; V1 came to final rest in northbound lane one. V1 was driven by a 24-year-old female who reported no injuries. She was unemployed and in town visiting her sister for the weekend. She claimed that she was intending on going straight through the intersection when the crash occurred. She reported her travel speed at 48 kmph (30 mph). She was listening to her sister (her right front seat passenger) read a map and failed to see the signal light was red. When she realized the light was red, she saw V2 coming from the left and applied the brakes and steered right, but was unsuccessful in avoiding the collision. She claimed that the traffic light was abnormally high and that contributed to her not being able to see it. She also claimed the sun was behind the traffic light which also obstructed her vision. Her vehicle was towed from the scene with disabling front-end damage. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V1 was "this vehicle traveling; passing through the intersection." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was an internal distraction, due to the conversation with the passenger. She was helping her sister read a map of the area and talking with her sister when the crash occurred. The driver was upset at the time of the crash due to family pressure to relocate. She reportedly had nasal surgery two months ago. Other associated factors coded to this driver include inadequate surveillance for not looking at the traffic signal sooner and an illegal maneuver because she ran a red light. V2 was driven by a 36-year-old female who was transported from the scene with possible injuries. She was on her way home from her second job when the crash occurred. She was unable to take any avoidance maneuvers and thinks she blacked out after the air bags deployed. The Critical Pre-crash Event for V2 was "other vehicle encroachment from crossing street, across path." The Critical Reason for the Critical Event was not coded to this vehicle. The driver was dialing her cell phone at the time of the crash; therefore, an internal distraction was coded as an associated factor. She also reported working 55 hours or more per week at two different jobs. She stated she is required to work extended work shifts. Work-related stress was also coded as an associated factor.
